STDARGS.description:
Public API. The parser handle is a positive integer keyed under
^STDLIB($job,"stdargs",p,...); state is per-process and per-handle.

  $$new^STDARGS(prog,desc)                   — alloc parser, return p
  addflag^STDARGS(p,long,short,action,dest)  — register a flag
  addpos^STDARGS(p,name,dest)                — register a positional
  addsub^STDARGS(p,name,subParserHandle)     — register a sub-command
  parse^STDARGS(p,argline,.ns)               — parse argline into ns
  $$help^STDARGS(p)                          — formatted help text
  free^STDARGS(p)                            — drop parser state

Actions:
  store_true   ns(dest)=1 if flag seen; default 0
  store        ns(dest)=<next token>
  count        ns(dest)+=1 per occurrence; default 0; "-vvv" expands
  append       $increment(k); ns(dest,k)=<next token>

"--" terminates flag parsing — subsequent tokens are positional even
when they start with "-".

Sub-commands: when any sub is registered, the first non-flag token
must match a sub name; the remainder is re-parsed by the sub-parser
against the same ns. The chosen sub name is recorded as ns("__sub__").

Args source: $ZCMDLINE on YDB; an explicit string elsewhere.
Tokenisation is whitespace-only — quoting is the shell's job.

Errors set $ECODE to one of:
  ,U-STDARGS-UNKNOWN-ACTION,
  ,U-STDARGS-UNKNOWN-FLAG,
  ,U-STDARGS-UNKNOWN-SUBCOMMAND,
  ,U-STDARGS-MISSING-VALUE,
  ,U-STDARGS-MISSING-POSITIONAL,

params.name: prog
params.doc: program name (rendered into the help banner)

params.name: desc
params.doc: one-line description (rendered into the help banner)

returns.doc: positive parser handle; pass to addflag / addpos / addsub / parse / help / free

free.description: Idempotent. The handle must not be reused after free().

addflag.signature: do addflag^STDARGS(p, long, short, action, dest)

params.name: short
params.doc: short form including "-" prefix; "" if no short

params.name: action
params.doc: one of: store_true, store, count, append

params.name: dest
params.doc: ns(dest) subscript that the parsed value lands in

raises.doc: `action` is not one of the four documented values

params.name: dest
params.doc: ns(dest) subscript that the value lands in

addpos.description: Positionals are filled in addpos() declaration order.

params.name: name
params.doc: sub-command name (matched against the first non-flag token)

params.name: sub
params.doc: parser handle from a separate new() call — the sub-parser

addsub.description:
When a parser has any sub-commands, the first non-flag token
must name one — `parse` raises U-STDARGS-UNKNOWN-SUBCOMMAND otherwise.

params.name: argline
params.doc: the raw command line (e.g. $ZCMDLINE on YDB)

raises.doc: token starts with "-" but isn't a registered flag
first non-flag token doesn't match any addsub() name
a `store` / `append` flag has no value token after it
a registered positional was not supplied

parse.description:
ns is by-reference. On parse error sets $ECODE to one of the
documented codes; otherwise returns silently.

returns.doc: multi-line help banner — usage line, description, flags, positionals, commands

help.description: Lists usage line, description, then flags, positionals, commands.

STDASSERT.description:
Output protocol mirrors m-tools/^TESTRUN exactly so m-cli's `m test` runner accepts STDASSERT-driven suites unchanged: " PASS " (two leading spaces) " FAIL " " expected: " (nine leading spaces) " actual: " "Results: tests

passed failed" "All tests passed." -or- " test(s) FAILED." Suite shape: STDASSERTTST ; new pass,fail do start^STDASSERT(.pass,.fail) do tMyCase(.pass,.fail) do report^STDASSERT(pass,fail) quit

start.description:
Sets p=0,f=0. Call once at the top of every suite entry.
Example: do start^STDASSERT(.pass,.fail)

silent.description:
Internal helper for STDASSERT's own self-tests of negative
paths (e.g., proving fail() actually increments). Production
callers should not touch this. Auto-reset by start()/report().

report.synopsis: Print summary; halt with error if any failures.
report.description:
Emits the "Results:" line m-cli's runner parses.
Halts with non-zero status if f>0 — drives CI exit code.

eq.signature: do eq^STDASSERT(p, f, actual, expected, desc)
eq.description:
Args: p,f counters by-ref; actual,expected scalars; desc string.
Example: do eq^STDASSERT(.pass,.fail,result,"hello","greet test")

ne.signature: do ne^STDASSERT(p, f, actual, expected, desc)
ne.description:
Inverse of eq(). Useful for "must differ" assertions.
Example: do ne^STDASSERT(.pass,.fail,oldId,newId,"id rotated")

true.synopsis: Assert cond is truthy (non-zero numeric prefix).
true.description:
M truthiness: leading numeric prefix !=0 is true; else false.
Example: do true^STDASSERT(.pass,.fail,$data(arr),"arr defined")

false.synopsis: Assert cond is falsy (zero numeric prefix or empty).
false.description:
Inverse of true(). Empty string and "abc" are both falsy.
Example: do false^STDASSERT(.pass,.fail,$data(missing),"missing undef")

near.signature: do near^STDASSERT(p, f, a, b, eps, desc)
near.description:
Use for fractional comparisons where exact equality is fragile.
Example: do near^STDASSERT(.pass,.fail,sum,3.14,0.01,"approx pi")

raises.signature: do raises^STDASSERT(p, f, code, errno, desc)
raises.description:
errno is matched as a substring of $ECODE (M's "[" operator).
For YDB DIVZERO use "Z150373058"; for general "M9" use ",M9,".
Example: do raises^STDASSERT(.pass,.fail,"set x=1/0","Z150373058","divzero")

raisesUnwound.synopsis: Trap-resume target — also reached on no-error fall-through.
raisesUnwound.description:
Internal — never an external entry point. The linter sees it
as a label without formals; the `captured`, `errno`, `desc`
locals it reads come from raises()'s frame, restored intact
after ZGOTO unwinds to that level.

contains.signature: do contains^STDASSERT(p, f, haystack, needle, desc)
contains.description:
Empty needle always matches (M semantics).
Example: do contains^STDASSERT(.pass,.fail,output,"OK","status line")

len.signature: do len^STDASSERT(p, f, actual, n, desc)
len.description:
Caller computes the length; this just compares two integers.
Example: do len^STDASSERT(.pass,.fail,$length(s),5,"5-char string")

recordPass.description:
Internal — used by every assertion. Single point of truth
for the runner-visible PASS line.

recordFail.synopsis: Increment f and emit FAIL block.
recordFail.description:
Internal — used by every assertion. Three-line FAIL block
matches the m-cli runner's regex (runner.py:76-78).

quote.synopsis: Return s wrapped in double quotes for diagnostic output.
quote.description: Internal — used by contains() to make whitespace visible.

STDB64.description:
Five public extrinsics:
  $$encode^STDB64(data)      — standard alphabet (+ /), with padding
  $$decode^STDB64(text)      — decode standard alphabet
  $$urlencode^STDB64(data)   — URL-safe alphabet (- _), no padding
  $$urldecode^STDB64(text)   — decode URL-safe; padding optional
  $$valid^STDB64(text)       — well-formed standard base64 (with padding)

Algorithm: take 3 bytes (24 bits) at a time, split into four 6-bit
groups, map each via the 64-char alphabet. Pad with '=' when the
input length is not a multiple of 3.

Input is treated as a string of bytes (one M character per byte —
values 0..255 via $ASCII / $CHAR). On YDB UTF-8 mode, multi-byte
UTF-8 characters round-trip correctly when the producer and consumer
both treat the string as M-characters. Arbitrary-binary support
(always-byte semantics regardless of $ZCHSET) lands with STDCRYPTO
in Phase 3 via $ZCHAR / $ZASCII helpers.

params.name: data
params.doc: byte string to encode (one M char per byte)

returns.doc: base64 with '=' padding; "" for empty input

encode.description: Returns the empty string for empty input.

decode.synopsis: Inverse of encode(); accepts standard alphabet + '=' padding.

returns.doc: decoded byte string; "" for empty input

decode.description: Returns the empty string for empty input.

returns.doc: URL-safe base64 ('-' / '_' alphabet, no padding)

urlencode.description:
Uses '-' / '_' instead of '+' / '/'; drops trailing '=' (JWT
convention). Use urldecode() to invert.

urldecode.synopsis: Decode URL-safe base64; padding may be present or omitted.

returns.doc: decoded byte string; "" for empty input

urldecode.description:
Trailing '=' is stripped before decoding so input from JWT
producers (no padding) and Python's urlsafe_b64encode (padded)
both work.

valid.synopsis: True iff text is well-formed standard base64 with padding.

returns.doc: 1 iff well-formed; 0 otherwise

valid.description:
Length must be a multiple of 4. Padding ('=') only at the end,
at most two characters. Body characters must all be in the
standard alphabet. Empty string is valid.

STDCACHE.synopsis: m-stdlib — LRU + TTL cache over a caller-owned local array.
STDCACHE.description:
Public extrinsics:
  new^STDCACHE(.cache, capacity?, ttl?)  — initialise; cap=0 unlimited, ttl=0 no expiry
  put^STDCACHE(.cache, key, value)       — insert/update; touches recency
  $$get^STDCACHE(.cache, key)            — fetch; "" if absent or expired; touches recency
  $$has^STDCACHE(.cache, key)            — predicate; expired keys are lazily reaped
  remove^STDCACHE(.cache, key)           — delete one entry; idempotent
  clear^STDCACHE(.cache)                 — drop all entries; capacity/ttl preserved
  $$size^STDCACHE(.cache)                — current entry count
  $$capacity^STDCACHE(.cache)            — declared capacity (0 = unlimited)

Tree shape (caller-owned; pass by reference):
  cache("cap")        — capacity (0 = unlimited)
  cache("ttl")        — TTL seconds (0 = no expiry)
  cache("size")       — current entry count
  cache("seq")        — monotonic counter for recency tracking
  cache("v",key)      — stored value
  cache("ts",key)     — recency: this key's seq number
  cache("o",seq)      — reverse map: seq → key (for LRU eviction lookup)
  cache("ex",key)     — expiry seconds-since-1840-epoch (only when ttl>0)

LRU eviction: the smallest seq in cache("o",...) is the least-recently-
touched key. On capacity overflow, $ORDER picks it off in O(1) per evict.
Recency touch: on get/put, kill the old (seq → key) entry, bump seq, write
the new (seq → key) entry, update cache("ts",key).

TTL eviction: lazy. has() and get() check cache("ex",key) before returning;
if expired, remove() is invoked inline. size() is decremented at that point.
A get() of an expired key returns "" exactly like an absent key.

Time source: M's $H = "DDDDD,SSSSS" (ANSI standard). nowSec() collapses
this to seconds since 1840-12-31. No `$Z*` extensions; runs unchanged on
YDB and IRIS.

new.synopsis: Initialise cache with optional capacity / TTL.
new.description:
Both default to 0 (unlimited / no expiry). Idempotent — calling
new() on an existing cache wipes it.
Example: do new^STDCACHE(.cfg,128,300)  ; 128 entries, 5-minute TTL

put.synopsis: Insert / update. Promotes the key to most-recent. May evict.

get.synopsis: Return the cached value, or "" if absent / expired. Touches recency.
get.description:
Expired entries are reaped inline before the lookup returns.
Example: write $$get^STDCACHE(.cfg,"hostname")

has.synopsis: Return 1 iff key is present and not expired; reap if expired.

remove.synopsis: Delete one entry. Idempotent — no-op if key is absent.

clear.synopsis: Drop every entry; preserve capacity / TTL settings.

size.description:
Reflects entries actually in the cache; expired entries that have
not yet been touched are still counted until lazily reaped.
Example: write $$size^STDCACHE(.cfg)

nowSec.synopsis: Return seconds since the M epoch (1840-12-31).
nowSec.description:
Internal — drives TTL stamping and expiry checks. M $H is
"DDDDD,SSSSS"; we collapse to a single integer.

evictWhileOver.synopsis: Pop oldest entries until size <= capacity.
evictWhileOver.description:
Internal — driven by put() when capacity is finite. Picks the
smallest seq in cache("o",...), maps to a key, removes it.

STDCOLL.synopsis: m-stdlib — collections (Set, Map, Stack, Queue, Deque, Heap, OrderedDict).
STDCOLL.description:
All collections are by-reference local arrays owned by the caller.
The caller's variable is the collection; killing it disposes of
everything. No process-globals are touched.

Reserved subscripts inside each collection:
  ("v",...)    values / members / payload
  ("n")        cardinality counter
  ("h")        head index   (queue, deque)
  ("t")        tail index   (queue, deque)
  ("k",i)      heap-array of keys   (heap)
  ("seq",key)  insertion sequence number   (odict)
  ("ord",seq)  reverse map sequence -> key   (odict)
  ("nseq")     monotonic sequence allocator   (odict)

Public API — each entry point is a procedure call (`do …`) or an
extrinsic ($$ …) per the prefix below.

Set (unordered, no duplicates; subscript-as-value)
  do setAdd^STDCOLL(.s,value)
  $$setHas^STDCOLL(.s,value)              -> 0|1
  do setRemove^STDCOLL(.s,value)
  $$setSize^STDCOLL(.s)                   -> count
  do setClear^STDCOLL(.s)
  $$setNext^STDCOLL(.s,prev)              -> next member, "" at end

Map (string-keyed dictionary)
  do mapPut^STDCOLL(.m,key,value)
  $$mapGet^STDCOLL(.m,key,default)        -> value or default
  $$mapHas^STDCOLL(.m,key)                -> 0|1
  do mapRemove^STDCOLL(.m,key)
  $$mapSize^STDCOLL(.m)
  do mapClear^STDCOLL(.m)
  $$mapNext^STDCOLL(.m,prev)              -> next key in $order

Stack (LIFO)
  do stackPush^STDCOLL(.s,value)
  $$stackPop^STDCOLL(.s)                  -> top; "" if empty
  $$stackPeek^STDCOLL(.s)                 -> top without removal
  $$stackSize^STDCOLL(.s)
  do stackClear^STDCOLL(.s)

Queue (FIFO; head/tail indices)
  do queuePush^STDCOLL(.q,value)          enqueue at back
  $$queuePop^STDCOLL(.q)                  dequeue front; "" if empty
  $$queuePeek^STDCOLL(.q)                 front without removal
  $$queueSize^STDCOLL(.q)
  do queueClear^STDCOLL(.q)

Deque (double-ended)
  do dequePushFront^STDCOLL(.d,value)
  do dequePushBack^STDCOLL(.d,value)
  $$dequePopFront^STDCOLL(.d)
  $$dequePopBack^STDCOLL(.d)
  $$dequePeekFront^STDCOLL(.d)
  $$dequePeekBack^STDCOLL(.d)
  $$dequeSize^STDCOLL(.d)
  do dequeClear^STDCOLL(.d)

Heap (min-heap; numeric key, optional payload)
  do heapPush^STDCOLL(.h,key[,value])     value defaults to key
  $$heapPop^STDCOLL(.h)                   value at min key; ""
  $$heapPopKey^STDCOLL(.h)                min key; ""
  $$heapPeek^STDCOLL(.h)                  value at min key
  $$heapPeekKey^STDCOLL(.h)               min key
  $$heapSize^STDCOLL(.h)
  do heapClear^STDCOLL(.h)

OrderedDict (insertion-ordered map; update keeps original position)
  do odictPut^STDCOLL(.o,key,value)
  $$odictGet^STDCOLL(.o,key,default)
  $$odictHas^STDCOLL(.o,key)
  do odictRemove^STDCOLL(.o,key)
  $$odictSize^STDCOLL(.o)
  do odictClear^STDCOLL(.o)
  $$odictFirst^STDCOLL(.o)                first key in insertion order
  $$odictLast^STDCOLL(.o)                 last key in insertion order
  $$odictNext^STDCOLL(.o,prev)            forward step
  $$odictPrev^STDCOLL(.o,next)            reverse step

Edge cases:
  - Empty-string set members and map / odict keys are silently
    ignored on add / put: M's $order cannot enumerate the empty
    subscript, and the API would not be able to round-trip them.
  - Pop / peek on empty silently returns "". Callers gate on
    `*Size` when they need to distinguish empty from a stored "".
  - Heap keys must be numeric (compared with M's `<`).
  - All collections may be reset by `kill <var>`; every entry
    point reads counters via `$get(...,0)` so a fresh / killed
    variable is indistinguishable from an explicitly cleared one.

setAdd.description:
Empty-string members are silently ignored — the iteration
API uses $order, which cannot reach an empty subscript.
Example: do setAdd^STDCOLL(.s,"alpha")

setHas.synopsis: Return 1 iff value is a member of set s.

setRemove.synopsis: Remove value from set s; absent values are no-ops.

setNext.synopsis: Return the next member after prev in $order; "" at end.
setNext.description:
Pass "" for the first call; loop until "" is returned.
Example: set k=$$setNext^STDCOLL(.s,"") for  quit:k=""  ...

mapPut.description:
Empty-string keys are silently ignored — see preamble.
Example: do mapPut^STDCOLL(.m,"name","Alice")

mapGet.synopsis: Return value at key; default if absent.

mapNext.synopsis: Return next key after prev in $order; "" at end.

stackPush.synopsis: Push value on top of the stack.

stackPop.synopsis: Remove and return the top; "" when empty.

stackPeek.synopsis: Return the top without removal; "" when empty.

queuePeek.synopsis: Return front without removal; "" when empty.

dequePushFront.description:
Front is q("h")-1 after the push.
Example: do dequePushFront^STDCOLL(.d,"alpha")

dequePopFront.synopsis: Pop and return the front; "" when empty.

dequePopBack.synopsis: Pop and return the back; "" when empty.

dequePeekFront.synopsis: Return front without removal; "" when empty.

dequePeekBack.synopsis: Return back without removal; "" when empty.

heapPush.description:
Sort uses M's numeric `<` over keys. value defaults to key
when omitted so a heap-of-numbers form just works.
Example: do heapPush^STDCOLL(.h,3,"task A")

heapPop.synopsis: Pop value at the min key; "" when empty.

heapPopKey.synopsis: Pop and return the min key; "" when empty.

heapPeek.synopsis: Return value at min key without removal; "" when empty.

heapPeekKey.synopsis: Return min key without removal; "" when empty.

heapRemoveTop.synopsis: Remove the root, restoring the heap property.
heapRemoveTop.description:
Internal — called by heapPop / heapPopKey after the root
value has been captured. Replaces the root with the last
leaf and sifts it down; clears the array entirely on n=1.

siftup.synopsis: Restore heap order by walking i toward the root.
siftup.description:
Internal — used by heapPush. Compares h("k",i) against its
parent at i\2 and swaps until the parent is <= the child.

siftdown.synopsis: Restore heap order by walking i toward the leaves.
siftdown.description:
Internal — used after heapRemoveTop replaces the root with
the last leaf. Compares against the smaller of two children
and swaps while a child is strictly less.

odictPut.synopsis: Store value at key; create-or-update preserving position.
odictPut.description:
Sequence numbers are monotonic — an update reuses the
existing position; a new key is appended to the back.
Empty-string keys are silently ignored (see preamble).
Example: do odictPut^STDCOLL(.o,"name","Alice")

odictGet.synopsis: Return value at key; default if absent.

odictFirst.synopsis: Return first key in insertion order; "" when empty.

odictLast.synopsis: Return last key in insertion order; "" when empty.

odictNext.synopsis: Return next key (insertion order) after prev; "" at end.
odictNext.description:
Pair with odictFirst to walk forward; pass "" to get the
first key. Skips entries removed via odictRemove.
Example: set k=$$odictNext^STDCOLL(.o,k)

odictPrev.synopsis: Return previous key (insertion order) before next; "" at start.
odictPrev.description:
Pair with odictLast to walk backward; pass "" to get the
last key. Skips entries removed via odictRemove.
Example: set k=$$odictPrev^STDCOLL(.o,k)

STDCOMPRESS.description:
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-024
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-036
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-020
M-MOD-024 false positives: rc / out are initialised before every
XECUTE'd $& call but the analyser cannot follow flow through the
XECUTE indirection.
M-MOD-036 (XECUTE injection) is intentional: the XECUTE wrapper is
the only way to invoke $&pkg.fn from M code that tree-sitter-m can
still parse — same trick as STDCRYPTO. The XECUTE source is built
from a literal template plus a `sym` symbol that the M-side public
surface controls; no user data flows into the XECUTE string.
M-MOD-020 (by-ref formal not written) false positives: dispatch
helpers write to `out` via the XECUTE'd $& call.

Public extrinsics (output via .out byref; return 1=ok / 0=fail):
  $$gzip^STDCOMPRESS(data,.out[,level])           — RFC 1952 gzip
  $$gunzip^STDCOMPRESS(data,.out)                 — RFC 1952 gunzip
  $$deflate^STDCOMPRESS(data,.out[,level])        — RFC 1951 deflate
  $$inflate^STDCOMPRESS(data,.out)                — RFC 1951 inflate
  $$zstdCompress^STDCOMPRESS(data,.out[,level])   — RFC 8478 zstd
  $$zstdDecompress^STDCOMPRESS(data,.out)         — RFC 8478 zstd
  $$available^STDCOMPRESS()                       — ""=ok, else missing

Errors set $ECODE: ,U-STDCOMPRESS-CALLOUT-MISSING, (.so unloaded);
,U-STDCOMPRESS-BAD-LEVEL, (level out of range); ,U-STDCOMPRESS-LIBZ-FAIL,
(libz returned non-Z_STREAM_END); ,U-STDCOMPRESS-LIBZSTD-FAIL, (zstd
returned an error frame).

Levels: gzip / deflate accept 1..9 (default 6); zstd accepts 1..22
(default 3). Level 0 (no compression) is rejected to avoid surprise
pass-through.

Output cap: 1 MiB per call (YDB's max M-string length on this
build; declared in tools/std_compress.xc). Streaming for larger
payloads is queued.

Backend: $&stdcompress.<sym> → libz (gzip / deflate) + libzstd
(zstd). Source at src/callouts/stdcompress.c; descriptor at
tools/std_compress.xc.

Deployment runbook (full detail in docs/modules/stdcompress.md):
  1. tools/build-callouts.sh                  ; produce so/<plat>/stdcompress.so
  2. export STDLIB_LIB=<dir-of-so>
  3. export ydb_xc_stdcompress=<abs>/tools/std_compress.xc
  4. ensure libz.so.1 + libzstd.so.1 are on the loader path

available.synopsis: "" iff both libz and libzstd loaded; else missing list.
available.description:
Example: if $$available^STDCOMPRESS()'="" w "missing libs",!
Probes by attempting an empty round-trip on each backend.
Never raises — clears $ECODE on the way out.

libzLevel.synopsis: Validate libz compression level — 1..9 valid, else -1.

zstdLevel.synopsis: Validate zstd compression level — 1..22 valid, else -1.

preallocBuf.synopsis: 1 MiB pre-allocated output buffer for the C side to fill.
preallocBuf.description:
Internal — YDB callouts need the M-side string at full
capacity before the C side writes into it. $justify("",N)
allocates N spaces in one O(N) pass; the C side overwrites
the contents and updates ydb_string_t.length on return.
Capped at 1 MiB (YDB's max M-string length on r2.02);
matches the [1048576] declaration in tools/std_compress.xc.

dispatchC.description:
Internal — XECUTE-wraps $&stdcompress.<sym>(data,.out,lvl)
so tree-sitter-m doesn't trip on the $&pkg.fn syntax.
Returns "" on success, "MISSING" if .so unloaded,
"FAIL" if libz/libzstd returned non-success. Caller maps
status to the per-backend $ECODE tag.

dispatchD.description: Internal — same XECUTE-wrap rationale as dispatchC.

STDCRYPTO.description:
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-024
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-036
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-020
M-MOD-024 false positives: rc is initialised by every entry to
dispatch3 / dispatch4 before any read, but the analyser cannot
track flow through the $ETRAP indirection used to recover from
missing-callout failures.
M-MOD-036 (XECUTE injection) is intentional here: the XECUTE
wrapper is the only way to embed $&stdcrypto.<fn>() without
the tree-sitter-m grammar tripping on the package-prefixed
external-call syntax (open work in tree-sitter-m). The
XECUTEd command string is built only from a literal template
and a `sym` argument that the M-side public surface controls
— no user data ever flows into the XECUTE source. Same
pattern as STDXFRM's @expr indirection.
M-MOD-020 (by-ref formal not written) false positives: dispatch3
/ dispatch4 write to `out` by reference, but the writes happen
through the XECUTE'd command string, which the by-ref analyser
can't introspect.

Public extrinsics:
  $$sha256^STDCRYPTO(data)              — 64-char lowercase hex
  $$sha384^STDCRYPTO(data)              — 96-char lowercase hex
  $$sha512^STDCRYPTO(data)              — 128-char lowercase hex
  $$sha256Bytes^STDCRYPTO(data)         — 32 raw bytes
  $$sha384Bytes^STDCRYPTO(data)         — 48 raw bytes
  $$sha512Bytes^STDCRYPTO(data)         — 64 raw bytes
  $$hmacSha256^STDCRYPTO(key,msg)       — 64-char lowercase hex
  $$hmacSha384^STDCRYPTO(key,msg)       — 96-char lowercase hex
  $$hmacSha512^STDCRYPTO(key,msg)       — 128-char lowercase hex
  $$hmacSha256Bytes^STDCRYPTO(key,msg)  — 32 raw bytes
  $$hmacSha384Bytes^STDCRYPTO(key,msg)  — 48 raw bytes
  $$hmacSha512Bytes^STDCRYPTO(key,msg)  — 64 raw bytes
  $$available^STDCRYPTO()               — 1 iff stdcrypto callout
                                           is loaded

Backend: $&stdcrypto.<fn> → libcrypto (OpenSSL EVP_Digest + HMAC).
The C source is at src/callouts/std_crypto.c; the YDB call-out
descriptor is at tools/std_crypto.xc; the build harness is
tools/build-callouts.sh.

YottaDB ABI note — argc-prefixed C signatures: YDB's
$&pkg.fn(args) external-call ABI prepends an `int argc` to
every C entry point. The .xc descriptor still describes the
user-visible signature (sha256(I:,O:) etc.), but the actual
C function is `int crypto_sha256(int argc, ydb_string_t* in,
ydb_string_t* out)`. A wrong argc returns -5. The legacy
$ZF + ydb_ci form was abandoned because YDB r2.02's parser
rejects the `.var` byref-output syntax for $ZF.

Deployment runbook (full detail in docs/modules/stdcrypto.md):
  1. tools/build-callouts.sh             ; so/<plat>/std_crypto.so
  2. export STDLIB_LIB=<dir-of-so>       ; resolved by the .xc
  3. export ydb_xc_stdcrypto=<abs>/tools/std_crypto.xc
  4. ensure libcrypto.so.3 (or .so.1.1) is on the loader path

Implementation note — XECUTE wrapper:
M-side calls go through dispatch3 / dispatch4, which build the
"set rc=$&stdcrypto.<fn>(...)" command as a STRING and XECUTE
it. This serves two purposes:
  (a) sidesteps the tree-sitter-m grammar gap for the
      `$&pkg.fn` external-call syntax (literal strings are
      not introspected by the parser);
  (b) sidesteps a pre-existing m fmt longest-prefix bug
      where bare $ZF was rewritten to $zfind / $ZFIND.
The XECUTE template is closed over a `sym` argument that the
public extrinsics control directly — no caller-supplied data
ever appears in the command source.

All error paths set $ECODE rather than raising directly so callers
can wrap with a single $ETRAP — matches STDCSPRNG / STDCSV style.

Out of scope at v1 (queued under T-N follow-ups):
  - AES-128/256-GCM encrypt/decrypt
  - Ed25519 / Ed448 sign/verify
  - X25519 key agreement
  - Streaming digest API (init/update/final tied to a handle)
  - SHA-1, MD5 (deprecated; ship only if a real consumer asks)
  - SHA-3 / SHAKE

sha256.synopsis: 64-char lowercase hex SHA-256 digest of data.
sha256.description:
data is treated as a byte string (one M character per byte —
values 0..255). Empty input returns the well-known SHA-256
digest of the empty string (e3b0c442...).
Sets $ECODE=,U-STDCRYPTO-CALLOUT-MISSING, if std_crypto
package is not loaded; ,U-STDCRYPTO-DIGEST-FAIL, if libcrypto
itself reports failure.
Example: write $$sha256^STDCRYPTO("abc")  ; "ba7816bf..."

sha384.synopsis: 96-char lowercase hex SHA-384 digest of data.
sha384.description: See sha256(); same contract, 48-byte digest hex-encoded.

sha512.synopsis: 128-char lowercase hex SHA-512 digest of data.
sha512.description: See sha256(); same contract, 64-byte digest hex-encoded.

sha256Bytes.synopsis: 32 raw bytes — SHA-256 digest of data.
sha256Bytes.description:
Use this when you need to feed the digest into another binary
pipeline; otherwise sha256() is more convenient.

hmacSha256.synopsis: 64-char lowercase hex HMAC-SHA-256 of msg under key.
hmacSha256.description:
key may be any length (longer than the SHA-256 block size of
64 bytes is fine — RFC 2104 specifies hashing the key down).
Empty key is technically permitted by RFC 2104 and produces
a deterministic result; do not rely on it for security.
Example:
write $$hmacSha256^STDCRYPTO("Jefe","what do ya want for nothing?")

available.synopsis: 1 iff std_crypto callout is loaded and resolves.
available.description:
Pre-flight probe — never raises. Use to gate code that needs
cryptographic primitives before any sensitive operation.
Example: if '$$available^STDCRYPTO() s $ec=",U-MYAPP-NO-CRYPTO,"

shaLen.synopsis: Digest size in bytes for the named algorithm.
shaLen.description: Internal — keeps the magic-number table out of the call sites.

dispatch3.description:
Internal — wraps $& in an XECUTE'd command string so
tree-sitter-m doesn't trip on the $&pkg.fn syntax.
Returns 1 on success, 0 on failure with $ECODE set.
isDigest selects the failure error code.

dispatch4.description: Internal — same XECUTE-wrap rationale as dispatch3.

zeros.synopsis: n NUL bytes — pre-allocates the O:ydb_string_t* output.
zeros.description:
Internal — YDB callouts need the M-side string at full length
before the callout writes into it; the C side updates the
ydb_string_t.length to the actual digest size on return.

STDCSPRNG.description:
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-024
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-036
M-MOD-024 false positives: the linter parses YDB OPEN/USE
deviceparams (readonly, nowrap, noecho) as local-variable reads,
then cascades read-of-undefined complaints across bytes/available;
rc / out are also initialised before the XECUTE'd $ZF dispatch
but the analyser cannot follow flow through XECUTE indirection.
Same finding as STDCSV / STDCRYPTO — tracked as a P2 in
TOOLCHAIN-FINDINGS.md.
M-MOD-036 (XECUTE injection) is intentional: the XECUTE wrapper
is the only way to invoke $ZF without m fmt's abbreviation
expander mangling the token (longest-prefix match against
$ZFIND). Same trick as STDCRYPTO / STDHTTP / STDCOMPRESS;
the XECUTE source is built from a literal template only.

Public extrinsics:
  $$bytes^STDCSPRNG(n)        — n random bytes
  $$hex^STDCSPRNG(n)          — 2n lowercase hex chars (n bytes hex-encoded)
  $$base64^STDCSPRNG(n)       — URL-safe base64 of n bytes (no padding)
  $$token^STDCSPRNG(n)        — n-char URL-safe token from [A-Za-z0-9_-]
  $$int^STDCSPRNG(min,max)    — uniform integer in [min,max] (inclusive)
  $$uuid4^STDCSPRNG()         — crypto-strong RFC-4122 v4 UUID
  $$available^STDCSPRNG()     — 1 iff /dev/urandom is readable
  $$useCallout^STDCSPRNG()    — 1 iff cs_random callout is loaded

Entropy: Linux kernel ChaCha20 CSPRNG. Two backends share the
same pool, so the choice is purely a perf concern:
  • cs_random — $ZF → getrandom(2). Batched single-call read;
    no fd churn, no record-terminator dance. Picked when
    $ZTRNLNM("ydb_xc_std_csprng") is set (descriptor deployed)
    and the .so resolves on first probe.
  • /dev/urandom — pure-M READ *b loop; one device read per
    byte. Always available on Linux YDB; the soft-fall-back
    when the callout is absent.
The public API is identical across both — callers never need to
pick. Suitable for session tokens, password reset tokens, JWT
signing salts, nonces.

Distinct from $RANDOM (Mersenne Twister): $RANDOM is fast and
statistically uniform but its output is predictable from a few
samples — never use it for security-sensitive identifiers. Use
STDCSPRNG instead.

Deployment runbook (full detail in docs/modules/stdcsprng.md):
  1. tools/build-callouts.sh       ; produce so/<plat>/cs_random.so
  2. export STDLIB_LIB=<dir-of-so> ; substituted into std_csprng.xc
  3. export ydb_xc_std_csprng=<abs>/tools/std_csprng.xc
With those unset, bytes() / hex() / base64() / token() / int() /
uuid4() all keep working via /dev/urandom — the callout is a
perf-only swap.

bytes.synopsis: Return n random bytes from the kernel CSPRNG.
bytes.description:
For n=0 returns "". May contain any byte value 0..255.
Tries $ZF → cs_random (getrandom(2)) first; falls back
to a /dev/urandom READ *b loop when the callout descriptor
is unset or the .so does not resolve. Both paths read from
the same kernel ChaCha20 pool — the choice is a perf swap.
Sets $ECODE=,U-STDCSPRNG-BAD-COUNT, if n<0.
Sets $ECODE=,U-STDCSPRNG-OPEN-FAIL, if neither backend can
produce bytes (callout missing AND /dev/urandom unopenable).
Example: set b=$$bytes^STDCSPRNG(16)  ; 16 random bytes

bytesFromDevice.description:
Internal — reads n bytes one at a time via READ *b so
record terminators (LF=$C(10), CR=$C(13)) in the byte
stream don't truncate the read. Sets $ECODE=,U-STDCSPRNG-
OPEN-FAIL, if the device cannot be opened.

hex.synopsis: Return 2n lowercase hex chars representing n random bytes.
hex.description:
Convenience wrapper: $$encode^STDHEX($$bytes(n)).
Example: set t=$$hex^STDCSPRNG(16)  ; 32-char hex token

base64.synopsis: Return URL-safe base64 of n random bytes (no padding).
base64.description:
Convenience wrapper: $$urlencode^STDB64($$bytes(n)).
Example: set t=$$base64^STDCSPRNG(32)  ; ~43-char URL-safe token

token.synopsis: Return an n-char URL-safe token from alphabet [A-Za-z0-9_-].
token.description:
Each character is one uniform draw from a 64-char alphabet
(6 bits of entropy per char), giving 6n bits of entropy total.
Sets $ECODE=,U-STDCSPRNG-BAD-COUNT, if n<0.
Example: set t=$$token^STDCSPRNG(22)  ; 22-char URL-safe token

int.synopsis: Return uniform integer in [min, max] (inclusive both ends).
int.description:
Uses rejection sampling on the smallest power of 256 covering
the range, so the distribution is unbiased.
Sets $ECODE=,U-STDCSPRNG-BAD-RANGE, if max<min.
Range is bounded by M scalar precision (~10^18 ≈ 2^60); do
not pass spans larger than 2^53 if exact uniformity is required.
Example: set d=$$int^STDCSPRNG(1,6)  ; fair 6-sided die

uuid4.synopsis: Return a cryptographically strong RFC-4122 v4 UUID.
uuid4.description:
122 bits of entropy from /dev/urandom. Same canonical 36-char
hex form as STDUUID's $$v4 — but use this when the UUID is a
security boundary (session tokens, signed-URL nonces, JWT jti).
Example: set id=$$uuid4^STDCSPRNG()

available.synopsis: Return 1 iff /dev/urandom is openable for reading; else 0.
available.description:
Pre-flight probe — never raises. Useful for guarding code that
needs CSPRNG entropy before any sensitive operation begins.
Returns 1 whenever /dev/urandom is readable, irrespective of
whether the cs_random callout is loaded (the device is the
always-available soft-fall-back; see useCallout() to detect
the perf-tier backend separately).
Example: if '$$available^STDCSPRNG() set $ecode=",U-MYAPP-NO-CSPRNG,"

useCallout.synopsis: Return 1 iff the cs_random callout resolves; else 0.
useCallout.description:
Pre-flight probe for the $ZF → getrandom(2) backend.
Never raises — clears $ECODE on the way out. Cheap fast
path: if $ZTRNLNM("ydb_xc_std_csprng") is empty the
descriptor isn't deployed — return 0 without paying the
XECUTE / $ZF round-trip. Otherwise issues a 1-byte probe
through the callout; returns 1 only when the .so loaded
and getrandom(2) succeeded.
Example:
if $$useCallout^STDCSPRNG() write "fast path"

dispatchRandom.synopsis: Invoke $ZF("cs_random", n, .out). Returns out on success, "" on miss.
dispatchRandom.description:
Internal — XECUTE-wraps $ZF so m fmt cannot mangle the
token (longest-prefix bug against $ZFIND). Returns the
filled buffer on rc=0 (callout success); returns "" when
the descriptor isn't deployed, the .so doesn't resolve,
or getrandom(2) reports failure — the M-side caller falls
back to /dev/urandom on "". Same XECUTE rationale as
STDCRYPTO.dispatch3 / STDHTTP.dispatchPerform.

STDCSV.description:
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-024
M-MOD-024 false positives: the linter parses YDB OPEN/CLOSE
deviceparams (readonly, newversion, stream, nowrap, delete) as
local-variable reads, then cascades read-of-undefined complaints
through the rest of parseFile/writeFile. Tracked as a P2 in
TOOLCHAIN-FINDINGS.md.

Four public entry points:
  $$parse^STDCSV(text,.rows)    — text → rows(i,j); returns row count
  $$write^STDCSV(.rows)         — rows(i,j) → RFC-4180 CSV text
  parseFile^STDCSV(path,cb)     — read path; dispatch cb(row,.fields) per record
  writeFile^STDCSV(path,.rows)  — write rows(i,j) to path as RFC-4180 CSV

Behaviours (RFC-4180 §2):
  §2.1 — records separated by CRLF; LF-only and lone-CR are also
         accepted on input. write() emits CRLF.
  §2.2 — trailing line terminator on the last record is optional
         on input; write() always emits one.
  §2.3 — header rows have the same shape as data rows; the parser
         does not distinguish them.
  §2.4 — spaces inside fields are preserved verbatim.
  §2.5 — fields may optionally be wrapped in '"..."'; the wrapping
         quotes are not part of the value.
  §2.6 — quoted fields may contain ',', CR, or LF as literals.
  §2.7 — '""' inside a quoted field decodes to a single '"';
         write() doubles any embedded '"' and wraps the field.

Extension over the RFC: a leading UTF-8 BOM (EF BB BF) is stripped
from the input by parse(). write() never emits a BOM.

Errors set $ECODE to one of:
  ,U-STDCSV-OPEN-FAIL,

Input is treated as a string of bytes (one M character per byte —
values 0..255 via $ASCII / $CHAR). Embedded NUL bytes are not
supported (M strings cannot represent them portably).

parse.synopsis: Parse CSV text into rows(i,j); return row count.

params.name: text
params.doc: CSV document (CRLF, LF, or lone-CR record terminators)

returns.doc: number of rows parsed (0 if `text` is empty)

parse.description:
Strips a leading UTF-8 BOM. Quoted fields may contain commas,
CRLF, and "" escapes (RFC-4180 §2.5–§2.7).

write.synopsis: Serialise rows(i,j) to RFC-4180 CSV text.

params.name: rows
params.doc: by-ref local subscripted as rows(i,j) for row i, col j

returns.doc: RFC-4180 text with CRLF row terminators; "" for empty input

write.description:
Fields containing ',', '"', CR, or LF are wrapped in '"..."'
with embedded '"' doubled per RFC-4180 §2.7. Sparse columns
walk via $order, so ragged rows are emitted with as many
fields as are defined.

parseFile.synopsis: Parse file at path; dispatch callback per record.

params.name: path
params.doc: filesystem path to a CSV file

parseFile.description:
Reads `path` line-by-line, accumulating across record boundaries
when a quoted field contains an embedded line break (RFC-4180
§2.6). For each completed record, calls
do @callback@(rownum, .fields)
where fields(j) holds the j'th field (1-based) and rownum is
the 1-based record index.

writeFile.synopsis: Serialise rows(i,j) and write to path as RFC-4180 CSV.

params.name: path
params.doc: filesystem path; truncated if it exists

writeFile.description:
Uses STREAM mode so embedded CRLFs in quoted fields are written
byte-faithfully.

STDDATE.description:
Public extrinsics:
  $$now^STDDATE()                  — current ISO-8601 UTC ("...Z")
  $$fromh^STDDATE(h)               — $HOROLOG -> ISO-8601 string
  $$toh^STDDATE(iso)               — ISO-8601 -> $HOROLOG form
  $$strftime^STDDATE(h,fmt)        — format horolog per fmt
  $$strptime^STDDATE(text,fmt)     — parse text per fmt -> horolog
  $$add^STDDATE(h,dur)             — h + ISO-8601 duration -> horolog
  $$diff^STDDATE(h1,h2)            — h2-h1 -> ISO-8601 duration

Horolog forms accepted/emitted:
  2-piece: D,S         ($HOROLOG)
  3-piece: D,S,U       (with microseconds)
  4-piece: D,S,U,T     (with microseconds and tz offset in seconds)

Calendar: proleptic Gregorian. Day 0 = 1840-12-31 (M $HOROLOG epoch);
Unix epoch (1970-01-01) = day 47117. Civil <-> day-count conversions
use Howard Hinnant's "days_from_civil" algorithm — works for any
year in proleptic Gregorian.

Errors set $ECODE to one of:
  ,U-STDDATE-BAD-HOROLOG,
  ,U-STDDATE-BAD-ISO,
  ,U-STDDATE-BAD-DUR,

now.synopsis: Return current time as ISO-8601 UTC with millisecond precision.

returns.doc: ISO-8601 UTC: "YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS.sssZ"

now.description: Always trailing Z. Source: $ZHOROLOG (microsecond + tz pieces).

returns.doc: ISO-8601 rendering (precision matches piece-count)

fromh.description:
2-piece D,S        -> "YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS"
3-piece D,S,U      -> "...HH:MM:SS.uuuuuu" when U>0
4-piece D,S,U,T    -> "..." + "Z" or "+HH:MM" / "-HH:MM"

toh.synopsis: Parse an ISO-8601 string into $HOROLOG form.

returns.doc: 2/3/4-piece comma string; "" on parse failure

strftime.synopsis: Format a horolog per a strftime-style format string.

returns.doc: the rendered date/time

strftime.description:
Unknown directives pass through as "%X". %z emits +HHMM/-HHMM
(no colon) or "" if h has no tz piece.

strptime.synopsis: Parse text per format string into a horolog.

returns.doc: 2-piece D,S; "" on parse failure

raises.doc: parse mismatch or invalid date components

strptime.description:
Literal characters in fmt must match `text` exactly; only the
documented directives are honoured.

add.synopsis: Add an ISO-8601 duration to a horolog. Negative prefix "-P..." accepted.

returns.doc: 2-piece D,S after addition; "" on error

raises.doc: `dur` is not a valid ISO-8601 duration

add.description:
Calendar arithmetic for Y/M (with day-clamp on shorter months);
raw day arithmetic for W/D; second-arithmetic for H/M/S.

diff.synopsis: Return h2 - h1 as an ISO-8601 duration.

returns.doc: ISO-8601 duration; "PT0S" if zero; "-P..." if h2 < h1

diff.description:
Days carry into hours/minutes/seconds; never emits Y or M
(variable-length).

STDENV.synopsis: m-stdlib — .env file loader with typed accessors.
STDENV.description:
Public extrinsics:
  $$parse^STDENV(text, .env)            — parse .env-formatted text
  $$parseFile^STDENV(path, .env)        — readFile then parse
  $$valid^STDENV(text)                  — predicate (parse without populate)
  $$has^STDENV(.env, key)               — 1 iff key was set
  $$get^STDENV(.env, key, default)      — string fetch with default
  $$getInt^STDENV(.env, key, default)   — integer; default if missing or non-numeric
  $$getBool^STDENV(.env, key, default)  — bool from {true,yes,on,1} / {false,no,off,0}
  $$getFloat^STDENV(.env, key, default) — float; default if missing or non-numeric

Format (typical .env subset):
  <line>      ::= <comment> | <blank> | <pair>
  <comment>   ::= "#" .*           (whole-line; trailing not stripped)
  <pair>      ::= <key> "=" <value>
  <key>       ::= [A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*
  <value>     ::= <bare> | <dq-string> | <sq-string>
  <bare>      ::= raw chars (whitespace-trimmed)
  <dq-string> ::= "..." with \n \t \r \" \\ escapes
  <sq-string> ::= '...' (no escape processing — POSIX-style)

Out of scope (queued for v0.x.y under T22):
  - variable substitution (`$VAR` / `${VAR}` references)
  - export prefix (Bash `export FOO=bar`)
  - multi-line values (PEM keys etc.)
  - process-environment integration (write parsed env back into
    $ZTRNLNM space — needs setenv() callout from T15)

parse.synopsis: Parse .env text into env tree; return 1 on success, 0 on parse error.
parse.description:
env is by-reference; on failure, env is left in whatever
partial state the parse achieved (caller may want to KILL it).
Example: do  set rc=$$parse^STDENV(text,.cfg)

parseFile.synopsis: Read path via STDFS and parse the contents.
parseFile.description:
Returns 0 (with $ECODE set by STDFS) if the file can't be read.
Example: do  set rc=$$parseFile^STDENV(".env",.cfg)

valid.synopsis: Return 1 iff text is parseable as .env. Discards the result.

has.synopsis: Return 1 iff key is defined in env; else 0.

getInt.synopsis: Return env(key) coerced to integer; default if missing or non-numeric.

getFloat.synopsis: Return env(key) coerced to float; default if missing or non-numeric.

getBool.synopsis: Return env(key) interpreted as boolean; default if missing or unrecognized.

parsePair.synopsis: Parse a key=value line; populate env or set rc=0.
parsePair.description: Internal — called for non-blank, non-comment lines.

decodeValue.synopsis: Strip optional surrounding quotes and (if double-quoted) decode escapes.

decodeDqString.synopsis: Decode escapes in a stripped double-quoted value.

validKey.synopsis: True iff s is a non-empty .env-style key.
validKey.description: Internal — first char letter or _; subsequent letters/digits/_.

isNumeric.synopsis: True iff v is a non-empty canonical M numeric form.

lower.description: Internal — getBool uses this for case-insensitive matching.

STDFIX.synopsis: m-stdlib — fixture lifecycle and per-test isolation.
STDFIX.description:
YDB nested-transaction-based isolation. YDB enforces TPQUIT:
``tstart`` and the matching ``trollback`` MUST live in the same
routine frame. STDFIX therefore exposes only one-shot wrappers —
``with`` and ``invoke`` open AND close their scope before
returning. There is no standalone setup() / teardown() pair.

Public labels:
  with(tag,code)         ; XECUTEs code inside an auto-managed
                           transaction scope; rolls back on exit
                           and re-raises any error code raised
                           inside ``code``.
  $$active()             ; → 1 if any nested transaction is open
                           (any TSTART, not just STDFIX-owned).
  register(tag,setupCode,teardownCode)  ; declarative fixture
  invoke(tag,code)       ; fixture-aware variant of with(): runs
                           registered setup hook, then code, then
                           registered teardown hook — all inside
                           one rolled-back scope.
  cleanup                ; idempotent rollback of any leaked
                           transaction scope; safe at $tlevel=0.

State layout (under ^STDLIB($job,"FIX",...)):
  STACK,$tlevel = tag    ; one entry per open scope, set inside
                           the transaction so TROLLBACK erases it.
  REG,tag,"SETUP"        ; registered setup code
  REG,tag,"TEARDOWN"     ; registered teardown code

``trollback $tlevel-1`` rolls back exactly the level this frame
opened, so nested with()/invoke() pairs roll back inner-only —
bare ``trollback`` (which targets level 0) would unbalance every
outer transaction.

Errors set $ECODE to one of:
  ,U-STDFIX-EMPTY-TAG,
  ,U-STDFIX-UNREGISTERED-TAG,

with.synopsis: XECUTE code inside an auto-managed transaction scope.
with.description:
Opens a YDB transaction, sets the scope tag in the stack,
XECUTEs code, then ``trollback $tlevel-1`` to roll back
exactly this scope. If code raises, the trap rolls back the
scope and re-raises so the caller can observe the original
$ECODE.
Example: do with^STDFIX("scope","do migrate^DDL")

active.synopsis: Predicate — is any nested transaction currently open?
active.description:
Returns 1 if $tlevel > 0, 0 otherwise. STDFIX-managed scopes
also appear in ^STDLIB($job,"FIX","STACK",N) for callers
that need to distinguish their own from foreign transactions.
Example: write $$active^STDFIX()  ; 0

register.description:
Persists the code strings under ^STDLIB($job,"FIX","REG",tag,...)
so later invoke(tag,...) calls can run them. Empty tags raise
U-STDFIX-EMPTY-TAG.
Example: do register^STDFIX("db","do reset^X","do drop^X")

invoke.synopsis: Run code with registered hooks wrapping it.
invoke.description:
Looks up registered setup/teardown for tag, then runs:
setup hook (if any), code, teardown hook (if any) — all
inside one transaction that rolls back on exit. Raises
U-STDFIX-UNREGISTERED-TAG if tag was never register()ed.
Example: do invoke^STDFIX("dbReset","do tCheck^MYTST")

cleanup.synopsis: Best-effort rollback of any leaked transaction scope.
cleanup.description:
TROLLBACKs all the way to $tlevel=0 if any transaction is
open; safe to call at $tlevel=0 (no-op). Useful as a
defensive between-tests reset in runner code. Note: this
rolls back NON-STDFIX transactions too — call only at a
top-level frame that owns no enclosing tstart.
Example: do cleanup^STDFIX

STDFMT.synopsis: m-stdlib — printf-style formatter (subset of Python str.format).
STDFMT.description:
Two public extrinsics:
  $$f^STDFMT(template,a1,a2,...,a9)   — up to 9 positional
  $$fn^STDFMT(template,.args)         — keyed via local array

Format spec — a subset of Python str.format:
  {} / {N} / {name}                  — field reference
  {:s} {:d} {:f} {:x} {:X} {:o} {:b} — type
  {:>10} {:<10} {:^10}               — alignment + width
  {:*>10}                            — fill char with align
  {:.3f} {:.4s}                      — precision (rounding for f,
                                       truncate for s)
  {{ }}                              — literal { and }

Type defaults:
  no type / s   — string, default left-align
  d f x X o b   — numeric, default right-align
  f             — default precision 6 (matches Python)

Errors set $ECODE to one of:
  ,U-STDFMT-MISSING-ARG,
  ,U-STDFMT-UNCLOSED-BRACE,
  ,U-STDFMT-UNESCAPED-RBRACE,
  ,U-STDFMT-UNKNOWN-TYPE,

f.signature: $$f^STDFMT(template, a1, a2, a3, a4, a5, a6, a7, a8, a9)

returns.doc: the rendered template

raises.doc: referenced position has no supplied value

f.description:
Supplied args fill positions 0..N-1; unsupplied positions raise
$ECODE on lookup. The 9-arg cap is M's per-extrinsic limit; for
more arguments use fn() with a local array.

fn.synopsis: Named formatter (lookups in the passed array).

params.name: args
params.doc: by-ref local; lookups go to args(name) or args(N)

returns.doc: the rendered template

raises.doc: referenced field has no entry in args

STDFS.synopsis: m-stdlib — File-system primitives (text I/O, path manipulation, bytes).
STDFS.description:
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-024
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-022
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-036
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-020
M-MOD-024 false positives: the linter parses YDB OPEN/USE/CLOSE
deviceparams (readonly, newversion, append, delete, exception,
nowrap, noecho) as local-variable reads. Same finding as STDCSV
and STDCSPRNG; tracked as P2 in TOOLCHAIN-FINDINGS.md.
M-MOD-022: STDFS uses $ZEOF and $ZLEVEL throughout — both are YDB
extensions to the M standard. v0.2.x ships YDB-only by design (see
"Engine portability" in docs/modules/stdfs.md). The IRIS arm will
arrive when STDFS gets its $ZF→stat callout backend.
M-MOD-036 (XECUTE injection) is intentional in the *Bytes() dispatch
helpers: the XECUTE wrapper is the only way to invoke $ZF without
the m fmt abbreviation expander mangling the token (longest-prefix
match against $ZFIND). The XECUTE source is built from a literal
template only — no user data flows in. Same trick as STDCRYPTO /
STDCOMPRESS / STDHTTP.
M-MOD-020 (by-ref formal not written) false positives: dispatch
helpers write to `out` via the XECUTE'd $ZF call.

Public extrinsics:
  $$readFile^STDFS(path)         — read file as string (LF-separated)
  $$writeFile^STDFS(path,data)   — write data; overwrite if file exists
  $$append^STDFS(path,data)      — append data; create if missing
  readLines^STDFS(path,.lines)   — populate lines(1..N) from file
  $$writeLines^STDFS(path,.lines)— write lines(1..N) as LF-separated
  $$exists^STDFS(path)           — 1 iff path exists
  $$remove^STDFS(path)           — delete path; no-op if absent
  $$size^STDFS(path)             — size in bytes; -1 if missing
  $$basename^STDFS(path)         — last path component
  $$dirname^STDFS(path)          — parent path (or "." / "/")
  $$join^STDFS(left,right)       — POSIX path join (absolute right wins)

Byte-faithful I/O via $ZF -> libc read(2)/write(2) callouts (T13+T14):
  $$readBytes^STDFS(path)        — file content as bytes (no CR/LF normalisation)
  writeBytes^STDFS(path,data)    — write data verbatim; no trailing LF
  appendBytes^STDFS(path,data)   — append data via O_APPEND atomically
  $$available^STDFS()            — 1 iff stdfs.so is loaded

Text I/O semantics: file is read line-by-line and rejoined with LF.
Trailing CR (CRLF input) is normalised to LF on read; write emits LF.
Binary I/O (readBytes / writeBytes / appendBytes) preserves bytes
exactly — no LF added on write, no CR/LF stripped on read. Use these
for non-text payloads (gzipped data, binaries, signed blobs).

Path semantics: POSIX-flavoured. Trailing slashes on dirname/basename
follow GNU coreutils conventions: basename strips them, dirname keeps
the parent with its trailing slash collapsed.

Existence checks delegate to $ZSEARCH, which YDB resolves via stat()
on first call and caches per-process. remove() opens the file with
the DELETE deviceparam — succeeds for files; silently no-ops if the
file is already absent (idempotent contract).

Backend (Bytes API): $ZF -> libc open(2)/read(2)/write(2)/close(2).
Source at src/callouts/stdfs.c; descriptor at tools/std_fs.xc.
When the .so is unavailable the *Bytes() entries set $ECODE to
,U-STDFS-NOT-WIRED, and return; the text-I/O entries (writeFile /
readFile / writeLines / readLines) and append() keep working via
the YDB SEQ device — append() then takes the read-then-rewrite
fallback automatically.

Deployment runbook (full detail in docs/modules/stdfs.md):
  1. tools/build-callouts.sh      ; produces so/<plat>/stdfs.so
  2. export STDLIB_LIB=<dir-of-so>
  3. export ydb_xc_std_fs=<abs>/tools/std_fs.xc

basename.synopsis: Return the last component of path.
basename.description:
Trailing slash is stripped before extracting the last segment.
Root path "/" returns "/"; empty path returns "".
Example: write $$basename^STDFS("/etc/hosts")  ; "hosts"

dirname.synopsis: Return the parent path (everything but the last component).
dirname.description:
No-slash inputs return ".". Root "/" returns "/". Trailing
slashes are normalised first ("/foo/bar/" → "/foo").
Example: write $$dirname^STDFS("/etc/hosts")  ; "/etc"

join.synopsis: POSIX path join: absolute right replaces left.
join.description:
Empty operand drops out. Trailing slash on left is collapsed.
Example: write $$join^STDFS("/a","b")     ; "/a/b"
write $$join^STDFS("/a","/b")    ; "/b"
write $$join^STDFS("/a/","b")    ; "/a/b"

exists.description:
Probes via OPEN with timeout=0 inside an $ETRAP — succeeds iff
the path is openable. Avoids $ZSEARCH's per-process cache, so
a path created and removed inside one M process round-trips
correctly. The trap catches YDB's hard-error on missing files
(Z150379354) and unwinds via ZGOTO so the extrinsic returns
cleanly — same pattern as raises^STDASSERT (TOOLCHAIN P1 fix).
Example: write $$exists^STDFS("/etc/hosts")  ; 1

existsRet.synopsis: Trap-resume target; reached on success fall-through too.
existsRet.description: Internal — never an external entry point.

size.synopsis: Return size of path in bytes; -1 if missing or unreadable.
size.description:
Implemented via OPEN/READ-loop tally — does not depend on a stat
callout. Acceptable for routine-sized files; for multi-MB paths
prefer the future $ZF→stat backend.
Example: write $$size^STDFS(path)  ; 4096

readFile.synopsis: Return file content as a string (lines joined by $C(10)).
readFile.description:
Trailing CR on each line is dropped (CRLF normalisation).
A trailing LF is normalised away (round-trips with writeFile).
Sets $ECODE=,U-STDFS-OPEN-FAIL, if path is missing or unreadable.
Example: set body=$$readFile^STDFS("/etc/hosts")

writeFile.synopsis: Write data to path (overwrite if exists).
writeFile.description:
Empty data creates a zero-byte file. Sets $ECODE=,U-STDFS-OPEN-FAIL,
on open failure (typically a missing parent directory).
Example: do writeFile^STDFS("/tmp/out.txt","hi")

append.synopsis: Append data to path; create the file if missing.
append.description:
Sets $ECODE=,U-STDFS-OPEN-FAIL, on open failure.
Example: do append^STDFS("/tmp/log","tick"_$char(10))
Implementation: text-mode read-then-rewrite — readFile of the
existing content, string-concatenate data, writeFile back. The
trailing-LF normalisation that writeFile always emits is the
documented contract, so the native O_APPEND path is *not* used
here (it would leave an interior LF whenever the file already
ended with one, breaking readFile round-trip semantics). For
byte-faithful append at EOF use $$appendBytes^STDFS instead.

remove.synopsis: Delete path; idempotent (no-op if already absent).
remove.description:
Sets $ECODE=,U-STDFS-REMOVE-FAIL, if the open-with-DELETE fails
for any reason other than "file already absent".
Example: do remove^STDFS("/tmp/out.txt")

readLines.synopsis: Read path into lines(1..N) (1-indexed; CRLF normalised).
readLines.description:
Each line is one M string under lines(i). Empty file → empty array.
Sets $ECODE=,U-STDFS-OPEN-FAIL, if path is missing or unreadable.
Example: do readLines^STDFS(path,.lines)

writeLines.synopsis: Write lines(1..N) to path, separated and terminated by LF.
writeLines.description:
lines must be 1-indexed and dense (no gaps in $ORDER).
Sets $ECODE=,U-STDFS-OPEN-FAIL, on open failure.
Example: do writeLines^STDFS(path,.lines)

writeBytes.synopsis: Write data to path verbatim — no trailing LF, no transcoding.
writeBytes.description:
data is a byte string (one M character per byte, 0..255).
Overwrites any existing file. Empty data creates a zero-byte file.
Sets $ECODE=,U-STDFS-NOT-WIRED, when stdfs.so is unavailable;
,U-STDFS-OPEN-FAIL, on open(2) failure.
Example: do writeBytes^STDFS("/tmp/blob.bin",bytes)

appendBytes.synopsis: Append data to path via O_APPEND — atomic at EOF, byte-faithful.
appendBytes.description:
Creates the file if missing. data is a byte string.
Sets $ECODE=,U-STDFS-NOT-WIRED, when stdfs.so is unavailable;
,U-STDFS-OPEN-FAIL, on open(2) failure.
Example: do appendBytes^STDFS("/tmp/blob.bin",chunk)

readBytes.synopsis: Return file content as a byte string — no CR/LF normalisation.

available.synopsis: 1 iff the stdfs callout is loaded and open(2) is reachable.
available.description:
Returns 0 if the .so is missing, the descriptor is not exported,
or libc open(2) fails on /dev/null.
Never raises — clears $ECODE on the way out.
Cheap fast path: if $ZTRNLNM("ydb_xc_std_fs") is empty the
descriptor isn't deployed — return 0 without paying the
XECUTE / $ZF round-trip.

dispatch2.description:
Internal — XECUTE-wraps $ZF(sym, path, data) so the m fmt
token-mangler doesn't touch $ZF. Sets $ECODE on failure:
,U-STDFS-NOT-WIRED, if the .so is unloaded;
,U-STDFS-OPEN-FAIL, otherwise (the C-side classifies via
stdfs_lasterror but the M API surfaces a single error code
per the OPEN-fail convention used by the SEQ-device path).

dispatchRead.description:
Internal — preallocates a 16 MiB buffer (matching the .xc-
declared cap), invokes $ZF(sym, path, .out), returns the
filled bytes. Sets $ECODE on failure (same scheme as dispatch2;
,U-STDFS-READ-TRUNCATED, surfaces when the file exceeds the cap).

preallocBuf.synopsis: 16 MiB pre-allocated output buffer for the C side to fill.
preallocBuf.description:
Internal — YDB callouts need the M-side string at full
capacity before the C side writes into it. $justify("",N)
allocates N spaces in one O(N) pass; the C side overwrites
the contents and updates ydb_string_t.length on return.

lasterror.synopsis: Return the C-side last-error message ("" if none).
lasterror.description:
Internal — readBytes uses this to distinguish OPEN-fail from
READ-TRUNCATED. Soft-fails to "" if the callout is missing
(callers already classify that via the rc path).

STDHEX.synopsis: m-stdlib — RFC-4648 §8 hex encoding (lowercase by default).
STDHEX.description:
Four public extrinsics:
  $$encode^STDHEX(data)   — bytes → lowercase hex (a..f)
  $$encodeu^STDHEX(data)  — bytes → uppercase hex (A..F)
  $$decode^STDHEX(text)   — hex → bytes (case-insensitive)
  $$valid^STDHEX(text)    — predicate: even length, all hex digits

Algorithm: each input byte splits into two 4-bit nibbles; each
nibble maps to one of "0123456789abcdef" (or the uppercase form
for encodeu). decode reverses the process after normalising the
input to lowercase via $TRANSLATE.

Input is treated as a string of bytes (one M character per byte —
values 0..255 via $ASCII / $CHAR). Always-byte semantics
regardless of $ZCHSET arrive with STDCRYPTO in Phase 3.

params.name: data
params.doc: byte string to encode (one M char per byte)

returns.doc: lowercase hex (a..f); "" for empty input

encode.description: Returns the empty string for empty input.

returns.doc: uppercase hex (A..F); "" for empty input

encodeu.description: Returns the empty string for empty input.

params.name: text
params.doc: hex text (any case; even or odd length)

returns.doc: decoded byte string; "" for empty input

decode.description:
Tolerates uppercase, lowercase, and mixed-case input.
Odd-length input drops the trailing nibble silently — call
valid() first if strict validation is required.

valid.synopsis: True iff text is well-formed hex (any case).

returns.doc: 1 iff well-formed; 0 otherwise

valid.description:
Length must be even; every character must be 0-9, a-f, or A-F.
Empty string is valid.

STDHTTP.synopsis: m-stdlib — HTTP/1.1 client (track H3, target tag v0.4.0).
STDHTTP.description:
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-020
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-024
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-036
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-008
M-MOD-008: dispatchPerform mirrors the 11-argument C-side
http_perform signature; collapsing into an array would require
flattening on both sides without simplifying anything.
M-MOD-020: get / post pass `.req` to request as a structured
input — request reads req but never writes to it. The
analyser's "pass by value" suggestion is wrong for array-
valued formals (a value-pass would drop subscripts). The
pattern recurs in $$buildRequest($req) too — req is read-
only but must be a by-ref formal to receive the subscripted
tree.
M-MOD-024 false positives: rc / statusCode / respHeaders /
respBody / errMsg are initialised before every XECUTE'd
$&stdhttp.* call but the analyser cannot follow flow through
the XECUTE indirection.
M-MOD-036 (XECUTE injection) is intentional: the XECUTE
wrapper is the only way to embed `$&stdhttp.<fn>(...)`
without tree-sitter-m tripping on the namespaced $&pkg.fn
syntax. Same trick as STDCRYPTO; the XECUTE source is built
from a literal template only.

Two layers:
  1. Pure-M wire-format helpers (iteration 1):
       do parseStatusLine^STDHTTP(line, .s)
       do parseHeader^STDHTTP(line, .name, .value)
       do parseResponse^STDHTTP(raw, .resp)
       $$buildRequest^STDHTTP(.req)
       $$formatHeaders^STDHTTP(.headers)
  2. Network extrinsics via $&stdhttp.* -> libcurl (iter 2):
       $$get^STDHTTP(url, .resp)
       $$post^STDHTTP(url, body, .resp, contentType)
       $$request^STDHTTP(.req, .resp)
       $$available^STDHTTP()
    The .so is loaded on demand. When it is absent these soft-
    fail with resp("error")="STDHTTP-NOT-WIRED" and return 0
    so callers can degrade gracefully. Deployment runbook:
       1. tools/build-callouts.sh        -> so/<plat>/http.so
       2. export STDLIB_LIB=<dir-of-so>
       3. export ydb_xc_stdhttp=<abs>/tools/std_http.xc
       4. ensure libcurl is on the loader path
       (or run scripts/seed-callouts.sh — does all four)

Response array shape:
  resp("status")             ; numeric status code
  resp("reason")             ; reason phrase
  resp("version")            ; "HTTP/1.1"
  resp("header", lowerName)  ; header value (name lowercased)
  resp("body")               ; response body bytes
  resp("error")              ; "" on success

Request array shape:
  req("method")              ; "GET" / "POST" / ...
  req("url")                 ; absolute URL
  req("header", name)        ; case preserved on the wire
  req("body")                ; bytes
  req("timeout")             ; seconds; default 30   (iter 2)
  req("followRedirects")     ; 0/1; default 1        (iter 2)
  req("verifyTls")           ; 0/1; default 1        (iter 2)

Header lookup on the response side is case-insensitive (keys
are stored lowercased per RFC 7230 §3.2 — server casing is
not semantically significant). On the request side we pass
the caller's casing through to the wire for compatibility
with finicky middleboxes.

Bodies are M strings of bytes (one M character per byte,
values 0..255 via $ASCII / $CHAR). No transcoding; no
Transfer-Encoding decoding (libcurl handles chunking before
the body reaches M in iteration 2).

Pure-M throughout in iteration 1 — no $ZF, no $Z* extensions.
Runs unchanged on YDB and IRIS.

parseStatusLine.synopsis: Split an HTTP/1.1 status line into version/code/reason.
parseStatusLine.description:
Sets s("version"), s("code") (numeric), s("reason"),
s("ok") (1=parsed cleanly, 0=malformed). Tolerates a
missing reason phrase (RFC 7230 §3.1.2).
Example:
do parseStatusLine^STDHTTP("HTTP/1.1 200 OK",.s)
; s("version")="HTTP/1.1", s("code")=200, s("reason")="OK"

parseHeader.description:
Trims leading SP/HT from value. Header name preserved
as-given. Splits on the first colon only — colons in
the value (Date, ETag with port:host pairs, etc.) are
kept. Returns name="" / value="" if no colon present.
Example:
do parseHeader^STDHTTP("Content-Type: text/plain",.n,.v)
; n="Content-Type", v="text/plain"

parseResponse.synopsis: Parse a complete HTTP/1.1 response message.
parseResponse.description:
Splits raw on the first CRLF-CRLF boundary (or LF-LF if
the server uses bare LF), walks the status line and
header block, fills resp("status"), resp("reason"),
resp("version"), resp("header",lcName), resp("body").
Multi-value headers join with ", " (RFC 7230 §3.2.2).
Header keys are lowercased for case-insensitive lookup.

buildRequest.synopsis: Assemble an HTTP/1.1 request message from req array.
buildRequest.description:
Returns the wire-format request string (request line +
headers + CRLF + body). Synthesises Host: from the URL
when not provided. Adds Content-Length: when a body is
present and the header is absent.
Example:
set req("method")="GET",req("url")="http://x.com/a"
write $$buildRequest^STDHTTP(.req)

formatHeaders.synopsis: Join headers(name)=value into a CRLF-terminated header block.
formatHeaders.description:
Each line emitted as "Name: value\r\n". Walks $order so
output is subscript-sorted (deterministic). Caller is
responsible for the final blank-line boundary.

get.synopsis: HTTP GET shortcut. Returns numeric status code, or 0 on error.
get.description:
Drives $$request with method=GET. Soft-fails with
resp("error")="STDHTTP-NOT-WIRED" and returns 0 if the
libcurl callout is unavailable.

post.synopsis: HTTP POST shortcut. Defaults Content-Type to application/octet-stream.
post.description:
Drives $$request with method=POST. Soft-fails the same
way as $$get when the callout is unavailable.

request.synopsis: Generic HTTP request. Returns numeric status code, or 0 on error.
request.description:
Reads req("method"), req("url"), req("header",name),
req("body"), req("timeout") (seconds, default 30),
req("followRedirects") (0/1, default 1),
req("verifyTls") (0/1, default 1).
Populates resp("status") (numeric), resp("reason"),
resp("version"), resp("header",lcName) (case-insensitive
keys), resp("body"), resp("error") ("" on success;
libcurl error string or "STDHTTP-NOT-WIRED" otherwise).
Returns the numeric status code on a completed HTTP
exchange (including 4xx / 5xx — the caller decides what
to do with non-2xx); returns 0 on transport / TLS / DNS
failures or when the callout is unavailable.

available.synopsis: 1 iff the libcurl callout is loaded and curl_easy_init() works.
available.description:
Returns 0 if the .so is missing, the descriptor is not
exported, or libcurl is unavailable on the loader path.
Never raises — clears $ECODE on the way out.
Cheap fast path: if $$env^STDOS("ydb_xc_stdhttp") is
empty the descriptor isn't deployed — return 0 without
paying the XECUTE / $&stdhttp.* round-trip.

lower.description:
Header tokens are ASCII per RFC 7230 §3.2.6, so we only
need to translate A-Z. Avoids a STDSTR dependency for
this one tiny use.

requestHeaderBlock.synopsis: Build CRLF-terminated header block from req("header",*).
requestHeaderBlock.description:
Internal — libcurl's CURLOPT_HTTPHEADER consumes one
"Name: value" line per slist entry; the C side parses
this block line-by-line. Host: and Content-Length: are
left to libcurl (synthesised from CURLOPT_URL and
CURLOPT_POSTFIELDSIZE respectively); explicit caller
values override libcurl's defaults via slist precedence.

parseHeaderStream.synopsis: Parse libcurl's captured header stream into resp.
parseHeaderStream.description:
Internal — when CURLOPT_FOLLOWLOCATION is on, libcurl
emits headers for every redirect response. We keep the
final response's headers (last block before the trailing
CRLFCRLF) and feed it to parseResponse with an empty
body — the caller installs the real body afterwards.

preallocBuf.synopsis: Allocate an n-byte M string for $ZF output capture.
preallocBuf.description:
Internal — YDB callouts need the M-side string at full
capacity before the C side writes into it. $justify
allocates n spaces in one O(n) pass; the C side
overwrites and updates ydb_string_t.length on return.

dispatchPerform.signature: $$dispatchPerform^STDHTTP(method, url, headerBlock, body, timeoutMs, follow, verify, statusCode, respHeaders, respBody, errMsg)
dispatchPerform.description:
Internal — XECUTE-wraps the namespaced $&pkg.fn call so
tree-sitter-m doesn't trip on the syntax. Returns the
C-side rc on success, -99 if the callout is unavailable.
Fast path: if $$env^STDOS("ydb_xc_stdhttp") is empty the
descriptor isn't deployed — return -99 immediately so
request() can flip to "STDHTTP-NOT-WIRED" without
triggering XECUTE compilation.

STDJSON.description:
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-024
M-MOD-024 false positives: the linter parses OPEN/CLOSE
deviceparams as local reads (`(readonly)`, `(newversion)`,
`(exception=...)`) and treats `for ... quit:c=""` loops as
reading the iteration variable before assignment.

Public API:
  $$parse^STDJSON(text,.root)      — populate root, return 1/0
  $$encode^STDJSON(.root)          — serialise to JSON text
  $$valid^STDJSON(text)            — 1 iff text parses
  $$lastError^STDJSON()            — "line:col: msg" or ""
  $$type^STDJSON(.node)            — type label
  $$valueOf^STDJSON(.node)         — scalar string
  parseFile^STDJSON(path,.root)    — read whole file
  writeFile^STDJSON(path,.node)    — write whole file

Storage convention (one M tree node per JSON value):
  node="o"           object — children at node(key)
  node="a"           array  — children at node(i), i=1..n
  node="s:VALUE"     string — VALUE is the decoded UTF-8 byte string
  node="n:VALUE"     number — VALUE is the canonical numeric string
  node="t" / "f"     true / false
  node="z"           null   ('z' avoids colliding with 'n' for number)

Parser state lives in a local context array `ctx` passed by ref
through every recursive helper; no global writes during parse.
The last error message is stashed at ^STDLIB($job,"stdjson","err")
for $$lastError.

Errors set $ECODE to one of:
  ,U-STDJSON-PARSE,
  ,U-STDJSON-ENCODE,

parse.description:
Kills `root` first. On failure, $$lastError() holds the
"line:col: msg" diagnostic and the partial tree is killed.
Internal recursion can fire $ETRAP at arbitrary extrinsic
depth; ZGOTO N:label unwinds the M-stack to parse()'s own
level before the GOTO so parseFail's `quit 0` always
executes in extrinsic context (avoids M17 NOTEXTRINSIC).

valid.synopsis: True iff `text` is conformant RFC-8259 JSON.
valid.description:
Discards the parsed tree; returns just the validity bit.
Empty input is invalid (RFC 8259 §2).

lastError.synopsis: Return the message from the most recent failed parse.
lastError.description: Empty when the last parse/valid call succeeded.

type.synopsis: Return the JSON type label of `node` (or "" if undef).
type.description: One of: object, array, string, number, true, false, null.

valueOf.synopsis: Return the scalar value for s/n leaves; "" otherwise.
valueOf.description:
For s, returns the decoded string content; for n, the
canonical numeric string as parsed from the source.

encode.description:
Object members emit in M collation order (numeric subscripts
first, then string subscripts in byte order). A gappy array
(e.g. node(1) and node(3) without node(2)) sets $ECODE
,U-STDJSON-ENCODE, rather than inventing a `null`.
ZGOTO-trap catches errors fired anywhere in the recursive
descent and unwinds cleanly to encode()'s own frame so the
post-error `quit ""` runs in extrinsic context (avoids the
M17 NOTEXTRINSIC harness crash documented previously).
Note: there is a separate open issue where the trap fires
on inputs that *should* encode cleanly, returning "" — the
STDLOG-JSON FORMAT('json') tests are deferred pending that
fix. The trap itself is correct; the over-eager $ECODE
setting is in encodeValue/encodeObject internals.

parseFile.description:
Reads the whole file into memory then defers to parse().
On parse failure, $$lastError holds the diagnostic.

writeFile.description:
Truncates `path`. Caller's responsibility to validate the
tree shape; encode-time errors propagate via $ECODE.

initCtx.synopsis: Reset parser state to start of `text`.
initCtx.description:
Internal — sets src/len/pos/line/col fields used by the
peek/advance/raise helpers.

peek.synopsis: One byte at the cursor, or "" at EOF.
peek.description:
Internal — does not advance. Returns "" for EOF (which is
distinguishable from the NUL byte $CHAR(0) by length).

peekN.synopsis: Up to n bytes at the cursor (may be shorter at EOF).
peekN.description: Internal — used to match multi-byte literals like "true".

advance.synopsis: Move cursor n bytes forward; track line/col.
advance.description:
Internal — newlines bump line and reset col; everything
else bumps col.

skipWs.synopsis: Consume RFC-8259 §2 whitespace (sp / ht / lf / cr).
skipWs.description: Internal — runs to first non-whitespace or EOF.

raise.synopsis: Stash msg with line:col prefix; set $ECODE; unwind.
raise.description:
Internal — every parse helper checks $ECODE after a recursive
call and quits early to let the top-level $etrap catch.

parseValue.synopsis: Dispatch on the first byte; populate `node`.
parseValue.description:
Internal — top-level parser entry. Must be preceded by
skipWs by the caller (parseObject/parseArray do this).

parseLiteral.description: Internal — used for the three keyword literals true/false/null.

parseObject.description:
Internal — handles empty object, comma-separated members,
empty-string keys (RFC 8259 allows them). Recurses into a
non-subscripted local (`tmp`) and merges back into
node(key) afterwards; passing subscripted formals by
reference (`do parseValue(.ctx,.node(key))`) is invalid
YDB syntax — only whole locals can be passed `.byref`.

parseString.description:
Internal — uses parseStringValue() to decode; wraps in
the s: sigil.

parseStringValue.synopsis: Parse "..." and return the decoded content.
parseStringValue.description:
Internal — handles \\\\ \\" \\/ \\b \\f \\n \\r \\t and \\uXXXX
escapes, including UTF-16 surrogate pairs. Bare control
bytes 0x00..0x1F are rejected.

parseHex4.synopsis: Parse exactly 4 hex digits; return codepoint or -1.
parseHex4.description: Internal — does not raise; caller decides what to do with -1.

hexVal.synopsis: Map a hex char to 0..15; -1 if not hex.

emitUtf8.description:
Internal — assumes cp is a valid scalar (caller filters
out the surrogate range D800-DFFF).

parseNumber.description:
Internal — captures the source span verbatim; rejects
leading zeros, lone decimals, and missing exponent digits.

encodeValue.synopsis: Recursive walker — return JSON text for `node`.
encodeValue.description:
Internal — dispatches on the type sigil; raises
,U-STDJSON-ENCODE, on unknown sigil.

encodeObject.description:
Internal — walks node() children in M collation order.
Uses `merge tmp=node(k)` to copy the child subtree into a
non-subscripted local before recursing; passing subscripted
formals by reference (`$$encodeValue(.node(k))`) is invalid
YDB syntax — only whole locals can be passed `.byref`.

encodeArray.description:
Internal — expects 1..n contiguous indices; sets
,U-STDJSON-ENCODE, on a gap. Uses merge-into-local before
recursing for the same reason as encodeObject.

encodeString.synopsis: Wrap `s` in quotes; re-escape per RFC 8259 §7.
encodeString.description:
Internal — bytes 0x00-0x1F lacking a named escape become
\\u00XX; bytes 0x20+ pass through (caller is assumed to be
handing in a UTF-8 byte sequence).

hex2.synopsis: Two-digit lowercase hex for a byte value 0..255.

STDLOG.description:
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-024
M-MOD-024 false positives: the linter parses YDB OPEN deviceparams
(e.g. APPEND) in writeLine() as local-variable reads. Same cause as
STDCSV's file-wide disable; tracked in TOOLCHAIN-FINDINGS.md.

Public entry points:
  DEBUG^STDLOG(event,k1,v1,...,k5,v5)   — up to 5 kv pairs
  INFO^STDLOG(event,...)
  WARN^STDLOG(event,...)
  ERROR^STDLOG(event,...)
  FATAL^STDLOG(event,...)
  LEVEL^STDLOG(threshold)               — "DEBUG"|"INFO"|"WARN"|"ERROR"|"FATAL"
  SINK^STDLOG(target)                   — "stderr"|"stdout"|"global"|"global:^GREF"
  FORMAT^STDLOG(name)                   — "kv" (default) | "json"

Output line format (kv, default):
  <ISO-8601 UTC ts> level=<NAME> event=<event> k=v k=v ...

Output line format (json):
  {"ts":"<ISO-8601>","level":"<NAME>","event":"<event>","k":"v",...}
  All values are emitted as JSON strings (preserves the kv contract
  that values are opaque text). Built via $$encode^STDJSON, so the
  line is byte-exactly conformant RFC 8259.

Value escaping (kv): a value with no space, '=', '"', or '\' is
emitted raw. Otherwise it is wrapped in double quotes, with embedded
'\' doubled to '\\' and embedded '"' escaped to '\"'.

Defaults: threshold=INFO, sink=stderr, format=kv. Configuration is
process-local (held under ^STDLIB($job,"stdlog","...")).

Errors set $ECODE to one of:
  ,U-STDLOG-INVALID-LEVEL,
  ,U-STDLOG-INVALID-SINK,
  ,U-STDLOG-INVALID-FORMAT,

Timestamp source: $$now^STDDATE() (millisecond-precision ISO-8601
UTC ending in Z). v0.0.4 shipped an inline helper; track L4b
(this commit) bumps to STDDATE now that v0.0.5 is in.

DEBUG.signature: do DEBUG^STDLOG(event, k1, v1, k2, v2, k3, v3, k4, v4, k5, v5)

params.name: event
params.doc: short event name (one token, no spaces ideally)

DEBUG.description: Suppressed when the configured threshold is above DEBUG.

INFO.signature: do INFO^STDLOG(event, k1, v1, k2, v2, k3, v3, k4, v4, k5, v5)
INFO.description: Emitted when threshold is INFO or below (DEBUG). The default level.

WARN.signature: do WARN^STDLOG(event, k1, v1, k2, v2, k3, v3, k4, v4, k5, v5)
WARN.description: Suppressed when threshold is ERROR or higher.

ERROR.signature: do ERROR^STDLOG(event, k1, v1, k2, v2, k3, v3, k4, v4, k5, v5)

FATAL.signature: do FATAL^STDLOG(event, k1, v1, k2, v2, k3, v3, k4, v4, k5, v5)
FATAL.description: Always emitted (threshold cannot be set above FATAL).

LEVEL.synopsis: Set the runtime threshold. Levels at or above pass.

raises.doc: `threshold` is not one of the five names

examples: do LEVEL^STDLOG("WARN")  ; suppress DEBUG and INFO
LEVEL.description:
Numeric ranks: DEBUG=10, INFO=20, WARN=30, ERROR=40, FATAL=50.
An emitter at rank n is suppressed when threshold > n.

raises.doc: `target` is not one of the documented forms

SINK.description:
"stderr" / "stdout" — write to /dev/stderr or current device.
"global" — write to ^STDLIB($job,"stdlog","buf",N).
"global:^FOO" — write to ^FOO(N) using $increment for N.

FORMAT.description:
kv format: <ts> level=<NAME> event=<event> k=v k=v ...
json format: {"ts":...,"level":...,"event":...,"k":"v",...}
JSON output uses $$encode^STDJSON internally so every line
round-trips through $$parse^STDJSON without loss. All kv values
render as JSON strings (matches the kv-line semantic that
values are opaque text); callers wanting typed JSON should
build a tree directly and call $$encode^STDJSON themselves.

STDMATH.synopsis: m-stdlib — Numeric helpers (clamp / min / max / sum / count / mean over arrays).
STDMATH.description:
Public extrinsics:
  $$clamp^STDMATH(x,lo,hi)     — clamp scalar x into [lo, hi]
  $$min^STDMATH(.arr)          — smallest value in arr (1st-level $ORDER walk)
  $$max^STDMATH(.arr)          — largest value in arr
  $$sum^STDMATH(.arr)          — sum of arr's values (unary-+ coercion)
  $$count^STDMATH(.arr)        — number of $ORDER-visible values at depth 1
