  $$mean^STDMATH(.arr)         — sum / count; "" on empty (no /0)

All array-walking entry points operate on the FIRST subscript level
only (the canonical "1-D vector" shape). Subscripts are walked via
$ORDER so any subscript shape works (1-indexed, string-keyed, sparse
integer keys, etc.). Multi-dim arrays read only their first level
— descend yourself if you want a deeper walk.

Empty-array convention:
  sum, count    → 0   (additive identity)
  min, max, mean → "" (no value to report; mean avoids /0)

Non-numeric values are coerced via M's standard unary-`+` rule:
  +"abc"=0, +"3.14"=3.14, +""=0, +"42-extra"=42. This matches
  how every other M arithmetic primitive treats string operands.

Pure-M throughout — no $Z* extensions, no STDREGEX dep. Runs
unchanged on YDB and IRIS.

clamp.synopsis: Clamp x into [lo, hi]. Returns lo if x<lo, hi if x>hi, else x.
clamp.description:
Caller is responsible for lo ≤ hi; the function does not validate.
Example: write $$clamp^STDMATH(99,0,10)  ; 10

min.synopsis: Smallest value in arr (1st-level $ORDER walk). "" if empty.
min.description: Example: new a  set a(1)=3,a(2)=1,a(3)=4  write $$min^STDMATH(.a)  ; 1

max.synopsis: Largest value in arr. "" if empty.
max.description: Example: new a  set a(1)=3,a(2)=9,a(3)=4  write $$max^STDMATH(.a)  ; 9

sum.synopsis: Sum of arr's values (unary-+ coercion). 0 if empty.
sum.description: Example: new a  set a(1)=10,a(2)=-3,a(3)=5  write $$sum^STDMATH(.a)  ; 12

count.synopsis: Number of $ORDER-visible values at depth 1. 0 if empty.
count.description: Example: new a  set a(1)=10,a("k")=20  write $$count^STDMATH(.a)  ; 2

mean.synopsis: Arithmetic mean = sum / count. "" if arr is empty (no /0).
mean.description: Example: new a  set a(1)=2,a(2)=4,a(3)=6  write $$mean^STDMATH(.a)  ; 4

STDMOCK.synopsis: m-stdlib — opt-in test-time call interception (mock registry).
STDMOCK.description:
Three procedure-form labels:
  register^STDMOCK(target,replacement)  — record a redirect
  unregister^STDMOCK(target)            — remove one redirect
  clear^STDMOCK                         — wipe every registration

Three extrinsics:
  $$resolve^STDMOCK(target)             — replacement, or target
  $$called^STDMOCK(target)              — call count since clear
  $$args^STDMOCK(target,n,i)            — arg i of call n

And one procedure that exercises the redirect + records the call:
  invoke^STDMOCK(target,.args)          — record + call resolve(target)

Mechanism — opt-in at the call site. Production code that wants
to be mockable calls invoke^STDMOCK("LBL^ROU",.args) instead of
do ^LBL^ROU(.args), or uses do @$$resolve^STDMOCK("LBL^ROU")(.args)
for the same effect without recording. Tests register a stub and
the indirection picks it up.

Storage — process-scoped (no cross-process leakage):
  ^STDLIB($job,"stdmock","reg",target)        = replacement
  ^STDLIB($job,"stdmock","cnt",target)        = call count
  ^STDLIB($job,"stdmock","arg",target,n,i)    = arg i of call n

Note on transactions: the registry lives in a transactional
global, so a TROLLBACK reverts mock registrations. v0.1.2 does
not provide rollback-immune mocks. The m-cli runner clears the
registry between tests so cross-test leakage is also a non-issue.

register.description:
Procedure-form. Overwrites any prior registration for target.
Example: do register^STDMOCK("EN^DIE","stub^DIETST")

unregister.description:
Procedure-form. Also drops the call count and recorded args
for that target so a subsequent re-register starts fresh.

clear.synopsis: Remove every redirect, counter, and recorded args list.
clear.description:
Procedure-form. Idempotent. The m-cli test runner calls
this between tests so registrations don't leak across cases.

resolve.synopsis: Return the replacement if registered, else target itself.
resolve.description:
Single-level lookup; chains are NOT followed. If target is
registered to A and A is registered to B, $$resolve(target)
returns A, not B.
Example: write $$resolve^STDMOCK("EN^DIE")  ; "stub^DIETST"

invoke.description:
Procedure-form. .args is passed by reference to the resolved
target. Records the call (count + arg copy) before calling.
Example: new a  set a(1)=42  do invoke^STDMOCK("LBL^ROU",.a)

called.synopsis: Number of invocations for target since clear / unregister.

args.synopsis: Return arg i of call n for target; "" if absent.
args.description:
1-indexed for both call number and argument position.
Example: $$args^STDMOCK("LBL",1,2) — second arg of first call.

STDOS.synopsis: m-stdlib — Process / env / cmdline helpers (YDB-only v1).
STDOS.description:
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-020
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-021
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-022
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-023
M-MOD-020: splitArgs writes to its by-ref second formal `args` but
not to `s`; the by-ref analyzer flags every caller as a candidate
without seeing the `args` write inside splitArgs.
M-MOD-021/022/023: STDOS is a thin layer over $ZTRNLNM / $J /
$ZCMDLINE / ZHALT — all YDB extensions to the M standard. v0.2.x
ships YDB-only by design; the IRIS arm lands when STDOS gets its
$CLASSMETHOD-driven helpers (T15, post-v0.3.0).

Public extrinsics:
  $$env^STDOS(name)            — environment variable lookup ("" if unset)
  $$pid^STDOS()                — current process ID (integer)
  $$cmdline^STDOS()            — raw $ZCMDLINE
  $$splitArgs^STDOS(s,.args)   — populate args(1..N), return N
  $$argc^STDOS()               — count of $ZCMDLINE arguments
  $$arg^STDOS(i)               — i-th $ZCMDLINE arg (1-indexed; "" out of bounds)
  argv^STDOS(.args)            — populate args(1..N) from $ZCMDLINE
  $$cwd^STDOS()                — current working directory (from $PWD)
  $$user^STDOS()               — current username (from $USER)
  $$hostname^STDOS()           — host name (from $HOSTNAME; may be "")
  exit^STDOS(rc)               — terminate the process with exit code rc

Argument splitting in v1 is whitespace-only — runs of spaces are
collapsed to a single separator and leading / trailing whitespace
is dropped. Quote handling (single and double quotes preserving
embedded spaces) lands in v0.2.y when STDARGS' quote-aware
tokeniser is back-ported. For now, callers that need quote-aware
parsing should pre-tokenise via the shell or use STDARGS directly.

env.synopsis: Return the value of environment variable `name`, or "" if unset.

returns.doc: value, or "" if `name` is empty or unset

pid.synopsis: Return the current process ID as an integer.

returns.doc: process ID
whole command-line tail (post-`-run ENTRY`), un-tokenised

splitArgs.synopsis: Tokenise `s` on whitespace; populate args(1..N); return N.

params.name: args
params.doc: by-ref local; killed then populated as args(1..N)

returns.doc: number of tokens

splitArgs.description:
Runs of spaces collapse; leading and trailing whitespace are
dropped. Tab and LF are NOT treated as separators in v1
(cmdline tails rarely contain them). Empty input yields 0.

returns.doc: count of whitespace-separated tokens in $ZCMDLINE

arg.synopsis: Return the i-th $ZCMDLINE argument (1-indexed); "" if out of bounds.

returns.doc: the i-th token, or "" if i is out of bounds

argv.synopsis: Populate args(1..N) from $ZCMDLINE; N is the implicit return.

params.name: args
params.doc: by-ref local; killed then populated from $ZCMDLINE tokens

returns.doc: value of $PWD; "" if unset

cwd.description:
For container environments where $PWD is unset, this returns
""; callers that need stat-based getcwd() should wait on the
$ZF→getcwd(2) callout backend.

returns.doc: $USER if set; otherwise $LOGNAME; "" if neither

user.description: Falls back to $LOGNAME if $USER is unset (System V convention).

hostname.synopsis: Return the host name (from $HOSTNAME) or "" if unset.

returns.doc: value of $HOSTNAME; "" if unset

hostname.description:
$HOSTNAME is exported by some shells (bash) but stripped in
minimal containers; callers that always need a value should
wait on the $ZF→gethostname(2) callout backend.

exit.synopsis: Terminate the YDB process with exit code rc (default 0).

params.name: rc
params.doc: exit code; defaults to 0 if unsupplied

examples: do exit^STDOS(2)  ; rc=2 to the calling shell
exit.description:
Implemented via ZHALT. The process exits immediately; no
$ETRAP fires, no cleanup runs, no further M code executes.

STDPROF.synopsis: m-stdlib — Wall-clock profiler with per-tag aggregates + percentiles.
STDPROF.description:
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-022
M-MOD-022: STDPROF uses $ZHOROLOG for microsecond-resolution timing
($HOROLOG is only second-resolution — too coarse for profiling).
$ZHOROLOG is YDB extension, also supported by IRIS — listed in
STDDATE's precedent. v0.2.x ships YDB-first; IRIS arm tracks the
$ZTIMESTAMP equivalent under STDDATE's IRIS pass.

Public extrinsics:
  new^STDPROF(.prof)                — initialise empty profiler
  start^STDPROF(.prof, tag)         — open a timer for tag
  stop^STDPROF(.prof, tag)          — close the timer; record sample
  $$count^STDPROF(.prof, tag)       — completed cycles
  $$total^STDPROF(.prof, tag)       — sum of elapsed (microseconds)
  $$mean^STDPROF(.prof, tag)        — total / count (integer floor)
  $$min^STDPROF(.prof, tag)         — fastest sample
  $$max^STDPROF(.prof, tag)         — slowest sample
  $$percentile^STDPROF(.prof, tag, p) — p-th percentile (0..100)
  $$tags^STDPROF(.prof, .out)       — populate out(1..N) with tag names
  clear^STDPROF(.prof)              — drop every tag's data

Tree shape (caller-owned; pass by reference):
  prof("active",tag)               — start time of an in-progress cycle
  prof("count",tag)                — completed cycles
  prof("total",tag)                — sum of elapsed microseconds
  prof("min",tag)                  — fastest sample
  prof("max",tag)                  — slowest sample
  prof("samples",tag,value,seq)=""  — per-sample, sorted by value

Time source: $ZHOROLOG = "DDDDD,SSSSS,US,TZ" (days, seconds, microseconds,
timezone). nowMicros() collapses to a single integer microsecond count
since the M epoch (1840-12-31). Sample resolution is microseconds; the
underlying system clock typically delivers ~1ms granularity on
container hosts and ~10us on bare metal.

new.description:
Idempotent — calling new() on a populated profiler clears it.
Example: do new^STDPROF(.p)

start.synopsis: Open a timer for tag. Stamps prof("active",tag) with $ZHOROLOG.
start.description:
Calling start() while a timer is already active is a no-op
(the existing start time is preserved).
Example: do start^STDPROF(.p,"db.query")

stop.synopsis: Close the timer; record one sample. No-op if no matching start.

count.synopsis: Return number of completed cycles for tag; 0 if untracked.

total.synopsis: Return sum of elapsed microseconds across all cycles; 0 if untracked.

mean.synopsis: Return total\count (integer floor); 0 if no cycles.

min.synopsis: Return fastest sample; 0 if no cycles.

max.synopsis: Return slowest sample; 0 if no cycles.

percentile.description:
p=0 returns min; p=100 returns max; intermediate values use
nearest-rank: ceil(p*N/100) into the sorted samples (1-based).
Example: write $$percentile^STDPROF(.p,"db.query",95)

tags.synopsis: Populate out(1..N) with tag names that have at least one cycle.
tags.description:
Walk order is M's $ORDER (alphabetical for plain string tags).
Returns N as the implicit value.
Example: do  set n=$$tags^STDPROF(.p,.list)

clear.synopsis: Drop every tag's data; preserves nothing.
clear.description:
Equivalent to new() — kept as a separate name so call sites
that read "clear" register the intent.
Example: do clear^STDPROF(.p)

nowMicros.synopsis: Return microseconds since the M epoch (1840-12-31).

STDREGEX.description:
Public API. The compiled-pattern handle is a positive integer keyed
under ^STDLIB($job,"stdregex",h,...); state is per-process and
per-handle. free() drops it.

  $$compile^STDREGEX(pattern)        — alloc handle, return h
  $$valid^STDREGEX(pattern)          — 1 iff pattern parses
  $$match^STDREGEX(h,s)              — 1 iff the entire s matches
  $$search^STDREGEX(h,s)             — 1 iff any substring matches
  $$find^STDREGEX(h,s)               — 1-indexed start of 1st match (0 = none)
  findall^STDREGEX(h,s,.out)         — out(n)=match-string for every non-overlap
  groups^STDREGEX(h,s,.g)            — g(0)=full match; g(k)=k-th capture group
  $$replace^STDREGEX(h,s,repl)       — every match replaced by repl (\1..\9 in repl)
  split^STDREGEX(h,s,.out)           — out(n)=segments between matches
  free^STDREGEX(h)                   — release state

Engine: Thompson-NFA on YDB; wraps $MATCH / $LOCATE on IRIS.
Pass A (this commit) ships the lexer + parser → AST. Passes B–E
land the NFA construction, simulation, capture tracking, and
findall/replace/split. Until then, the engine entry points
(match/search/find/findall/groups/replace/split) remain safe-
default stubs so the harness can report per-pass progress.

v0.2.0 supported subset:
  Literals, "." (any char except newline), "^"/"$" (string-anchor),
  quantifiers "*", "+", "?", "{n}", "{n,}", "{n,m}" (greedy),
  character classes "[abc]" / "[^abc]" / "[a-z]", predefined
  classes \d \D \w \W \s \S, escapes \\ \. \^ \$ \( \) \[ \]
  \{ \} \| \* \+ \? \n \t \r, alternation "|", grouping "(...)"
  (capturing) and "(?:...)" (non-capturing).

Out of scope at v0.2.0 — compile() rejects with U-STDREGEX-UNSUPPORTED:
  Back-references in the pattern (\1..\9), lookaround ((?=...),
  (?!...), (?<=...), (?<!...), named groups (?P<...>, (?<...>)),
  Unicode property classes (\p{...}, \P{...}), inline modifiers
  ((?i), (?m), …), possessive (*+, ++, ?+) and lazy (*?, +?, ??)
  quantifiers.
A follow-on STDREGEX_PCRE (Phase 3-adjacent) ships full PCRE
via $ZF to libpcre2.

AST representation. After a successful parse, compile() commits the
AST to ^STDLIB($job,"stdregex",h,"ast",id,...) under integer ids
allocated densely from 1; ^...,h,"root") names the root id. Each
node carries a "type" subscript:
  literal  — char            — "char"
  dot      — (no extras)
  anchor   — "sym" = "^"|"$"
  pred     — "sym" = d|D|w|W|s|S
  klass    — "negated" = 0|1; "item",N,"kind" = char|range|pred,
             with "char" or ("lo"+"hi") or "sym"
  star,plus,quest — "child" = childId
  range    — "min", "max" (max="" = unbounded), "child"
  concat   — "child",1..N
  alt      — "branch",1..N
  group    — "capturing" = 0|1; "groupNum" (when capturing); "child"

Errors set $ECODE to one of:
  ,U-STDREGEX-BAD-PATTERN,        — parse error in pattern
  ,U-STDREGEX-UNSUPPORTED,        — feature outside the v0.2.0 subset
  ,U-STDREGEX-NO-MATCH,           — groups() called but pattern did not match

compile.description:
Returns a positive integer; pass to match/search/find/findall/
groups/replace/split/free. Sets $ECODE to U-STDREGEX-BAD-PATTERN
on parse error or U-STDREGEX-UNSUPPORTED on a feature outside
the v0.2.0 subset.
Example: set h=$$compile^STDREGEX("\d+")

raise.synopsis: Raise a U-STDREGEX-<err> error code via a fresh frame.
raise.description:
Internal — fires the caller's $ETRAP from a nested frame so
that the trap's QUIT-with-empty-$ECODE resumes execution
at a known safe point in the caller (a guarded quit), not
in the middle of post-error cleanup.

free.description:
Idempotent. The handle must not be reused after free().
Example: do free^STDREGEX(h)

valid.synopsis: True iff pattern parses cleanly under the v0.2.0 subset.
valid.description:
Returns 1 for a parseable pattern, 0 otherwise. Does not
distinguish BAD-PATTERN from UNSUPPORTED — compile() does.
Example: write $$valid^STDREGEX("[a-z]+")  ; 1

match.synopsis: True iff the entire string s matches the pattern.
match.description:
Anchored on both ends — equivalent to "^pattern$" semantics.
Example: write $$match^STDREGEX(h,"42")  ; 1 if h compiled "\d+"

search.synopsis: True iff any substring of s matches the pattern.
search.description:
Unanchored unless the pattern itself uses ^ or $.
Example: write $$search^STDREGEX(h,"the 42 cats")  ; 1 for "\d+"

find.synopsis: 1-indexed start of the first match in s; 0 if no match.

findall.synopsis: Populate out(1..N) with every non-overlapping match text.
findall.description:
out is by-reference. After return, $order(out("")) walks the
matches in left-to-right order. Empty out if no match.
Example: do findall^STDREGEX(h,"a 1 b 22",.out)

groups.synopsis: Populate g(0..N) with the full match text and each capture group.
groups.description:
g is by-reference. g(0) is the full match; g(k) for k>=1 is
the k-th capture group counted by '(' position. Capture-group
numbering ignores (?:...) non-capturing groups. Sets $ECODE to
U-STDREGEX-NO-MATCH if pattern does not match s.
Example: do groups^STDREGEX(h,"42-foo",.g)

replace.synopsis: Return s with every match replaced by repl.
replace.description:
\1..\9 in repl are expanded to the corresponding capture group
text. \\ in repl is a literal backslash.
Example: write $$replace^STDREGEX(h,"x42y","[\1]")  ; "x[42]y"

expand.synopsis: Expand \\1..\\9 backrefs in repl using caps; \\\\ is literal.
expand.description:
Internal — used by replace(). Unrecognised \X is treated as
a literal \X (passed through). \\\\ becomes a single \\.

split.synopsis: Populate out(1..N) with the segments of s between matches.
split.description:
out is by-reference. Adjacent matches produce empty segments;
leading/trailing matches produce a leading/trailing empty
segment. Empty pattern is a parse error.
Example: do split^STDREGEX(h,"a,b,c",.out)

parse.synopsis: Parse pattern into ast(...); set root id.
parse.description:
Internal — returns "" on success, "BAD-PATTERN" or
"UNSUPPORTED" on failure. Does not set $ECODE; callers
choose whether to raise or to return a soft signal.

nextId.synopsis: Allocate a fresh AST id and stamp its type.
nextId.description:
Internal — every node-builder calls this first to reserve
an id, then writes its type-specific subscripts.

pAlt.description:
Internal — yields either the single concat node or an
alt node whose branches are the concats.

pConcat.description:
Internal — empty input is legal and yields an empty concat
(epsilon match). One element collapses to that element.

concatStops.synopsis: True if the next byte ends the current concat sequence.
concatStops.description: Internal — concat ends at end-of-pattern, '|', or ')'.

pAtomQuant.description:
Internal — wraps the atom in a star/plus/quest/range node
when a quantifier follows. Lazy '?' or possessive '+' after
a quantifier is rejected as UNSUPPORTED.

checkLazyPoss.description: Internal — v0.2.0 ships greedy quantifiers only.

pAtom.description:
Internal — the unitary regex element a quantifier can attach to.
Stray '*' / '+' / '?' / '{' / ')' / '|' here are BAD-PATTERN.

pEscape.synopsis: '\' followed by one char or short class.
pEscape.description:
Internal — handles literal escapes, predefined classes,
control chars, back-refs (UNSUPPORTED), and \p / \P
Unicode property heads (UNSUPPORTED).

pClass.description:
Internal — empty class '[]' is BAD-PATTERN. Items are bare
chars, escape-class items, or 'lo-hi' ranges; reverse range
'z-a' is BAD-PATTERN. Final '-' is a literal.

classItem.synopsis: Read one class item — bare char, escape, range, or pred.

classEscape.synopsis: Handle a '\<x>' inside a character class.
classEscape.description:
Internal — predefined classes (\d, \w, …) become "pred" items;
literal escapes become "char" items (and may begin a range).

classRangeHi.signature: do classRangeHi^STDREGEX(st, ast, id, n, lo)
classRangeHi.synopsis: Read the high end of a class range and append it.
classRangeHi.description:
Internal — accepts a literal char or an escape-via-literal
form. Reverse range (lo > hi by ASCII) is BAD-PATTERN.

pGroup.description:
Internal — '(?:' non-capturing; '(?=' / '(?!' / '(?<=' /
'(?<!' lookaround (UNSUPPORTED); '(?<' or '(?P<' named
capture (UNSUPPORTED); '(?[imsxn]' inline modifier
(UNSUPPORTED). Plain '(' is a capturing group.

pRange.description:
Internal — bare '{' that isn't a valid range (no leading
digit, missing '}', m<n) is BAD-PATTERN; literal '{' must
be escaped as '\{'.

isDigit.synopsis: True iff c is one ASCII decimal digit.
isDigit.description: Internal — used by pRange for {n,m} parsing.

buildNfa.synopsis: Build the NFA for handle h from its committed AST root.
buildNfa.description:
Internal — called by compile() once parse() has committed
the AST. Allocates state ids densely from 1; sets entry/exit.

newSt.synopsis: Allocate a fresh NFA state id under handle h.
newSt.description: Internal — every fragment-builder calls this for endpoints.

bld.synopsis: Compile AST node id; populate frag("entry","exit").
bld.description:
Internal — central dispatcher. Each AST node type has a
matching bld* helper that allocates fresh state ids and
writes its edges into the NFA global.

bldConcat.synopsis: A B C ... — chain fragments through ε-edges.
bldConcat.description:
Internal — empty concat (no children) yields a one-state
ε-fragment so callers can treat it uniformly.

bldRange.description:
optional copies. Test patterns use small bounds (n<=4) so
unrolling is fine; a future pass could special-case large m.

attempt.synopsis: Run NFA from startPos; return rightmost end pos.
attempt.description:
Internal — returns 0 if no match starts here, otherwise the
1-indexed position one past the last consumed char where the
accept state was reached.

step.synopsis: Consume char c from each active state; populate next.
step.description:
Internal — only consuming edges (literal/dot/pred/klass)
are followed here; zero-width edges are handled by epsClose.

epsClose.signature: do epsClose^STDREGEX(h, st, isStart, isEnd, close)
epsClose.synopsis: Add st + ε-reachable states to close.
epsClose.description:
Internal — iterative BFS through eps / anchor / capStart /
capEnd edges. Anchor edges only pass when isStart / isEnd
matches the symbol. close is by-reference and may already
contain other states; epsClose unions in.

predMatch.synopsis: True iff char c satisfies predicate sym.
predMatch.description:
Internal — \d/\D digit, \w/\W word (alnum + '_'),
\s/\S whitespace (space, tab, LF, CR, FF, VT).

klassMatch.synopsis: True iff char c matches the user character class at AST id.
klassMatch.description:
Internal — items live under ^...,h,"ast",id,"item",N. A
char hits if any item matches; negated classes invert.

attemptCap.description:
Internal — returns 0 on no match, otherwise the rightmost
position one past the last consumed char where the accept
state was reached. winCaps is by-reference and on success
holds (g,"s")/(g,"e") for every group plus (0,"s")/(0,"e")
for the full match.

stepCap.synopsis: Consume char c; carry cap state forward.
stepCap.description:
Internal — only consuming edges (literal/dot/pred/klass)
are followed; first-arrival wins for next-state caps.

epsCloseCap.signature: do epsCloseCap^STDREGEX(h, srcCaps, st, isStart, isEnd, pos, active)
epsCloseCap.description:
Internal — recursive DFS over zero-width edges in edge-index
order. First arrival at a state wins; capStart / capEnd
stamp pos into a fresh capture map before recursing.

STDSEED.description:
Public API:
  load^STDSEED(path,filer)         ; load TSV manifest via `filer`
                                     (default: fileViaDie -> FILE^DIE)
  $$loaded^STDSEED(path)           ; 1 iff path is currently tracked
  clear^STDSEED(path)              ; drop bookkeeping for path
  $$validate^STDSEED(path)         ; 1 if manifest parses; raises on syntax
  loadJson^STDSEED(jsonText,filer) ; load JSON-array manifest via `filer`
                                     (each element: {"file":..,"fields":{..}})

Manifest format (TSV, one row per record):
  <file>\t<field>=<value>\t<field>=<value>...
Lines beginning with '#' are comments. Whitespace-only lines skip.

Filer hook: the optional `filer` argument is a "tag^routine"
reference invoked once per row as
  do @filer@(file,.fda,.iens)
with fda(file,"+1,",field)=value and `iens` an output IEN. The
default — when filer is empty — calls FILE^DIE; that path
assumes FileMan is loaded in the runtime environment.

State per loaded path lives under ^STDLIB($job,"stdseed",path,...).
clear() drops it. STDSEED does NOT open its own transaction;
callers wanting rollback semantics wrap in STDFIX (v0.1.1+).

Errors set $ECODE to one of:
  ,U-STDSEED-FILE-NOT-FOUND,
  ,U-STDSEED-MISSING-FILE,
  ,U-STDSEED-MISSING-FIELD,
  ,U-STDSEED-FILER-ERROR,
  ,U-STDSEED-INVALID-JSON,
  ,U-STDSEED-INVALID-MANIFEST,

load.description:
Each parsed row is dispatched once. Errors propagate via $ECODE.
Example: do load^STDSEED("/etc/seed.tsv")

clear.description:
Best-effort — does not delete already-filed records (caller's
responsibility, typically via STDFIX rollback).
Example: do clear^STDSEED("/etc/seed.tsv")

validate.synopsis: Parse-only check — return 1 on success; raise on syntax error.
validate.description:
Never invokes the filer. Use as a pre-flight before load().
Example: write $$validate^STDSEED("/etc/seed.tsv")

loadJson.description:
jsonText is a JSON array; each element is an object with
required string key "file" and optional object key "fields"
whose members become FDA fname=fval pairs. Dispatched once
per element via `filer` (default fileViaDie -> FILE^DIE).
Parse failures raise U-STDSEED-INVALID-JSON; structurally
malformed manifests raise U-STDSEED-INVALID-MANIFEST.
Example: do loadJson^STDSEED("[{""file"":""PATIENT"",""fields"":{"".01"":""Smith""}}]")

walk.synopsis: Read TSV at `path`; dispatch per non-blank/non-comment row.
walk.description:
Internal — load() and validate() share this; doFile=0 skips the
filer call and the bookkeeping write.

processRow.synopsis: Parse one TSV row; build FDA; dispatch.
processRow.description:
Internal — sets $ECODE on syntax errors. When doFile=0 only
validates the row's shape.

dispatch.description:
Internal — wraps the filer call and translates any $ECODE the
filer raises into U-STDSEED-FILER-ERROR.

walkJson.synopsis: Iterate a parsed JSON array; dispatch each element.
walkJson.description:
Internal — tree is the root array node from $$parse^STDJSON.
Element shape: {"file":<string>,"fields":{<fname>:<scalar>,...}}

dispatchJson.synopsis: Invoke filer for one JSON element.

fileViaDie.description:
Internal default. Assumes FileMan available. After FILE^DIE,
^TMP("DIERR",$JOB) is checked; on error sets $ECODE which
dispatch() relays as U-STDSEED-FILER-ERROR. Real-environment
integration is the v0.1.4 follow-on; this label compiles and
runs against any FileMan host but is not unit-tested.

tryOpen.synopsis: Attempt OPEN with timeout; trap IO errors.
tryOpen.description:
Internal — sets opened=1 on success, 0 on any IO failure
(missing file, permission denied, etc.). Lets walk() decide
whether to raise FILE-NOT-FOUND.

trim.synopsis: Strip leading and trailing ASCII whitespace (space, tab, CR, LF).

STDSEMVER.description:
Public extrinsics:
  $$valid^STDSEMVER(s)         — 1 iff s is a valid SemVer string
  $$parse^STDSEMVER(s,.v)      — populate v(1..5); return 1/0
  $$major^STDSEMVER(s)         — major component, or "" if invalid
  $$minor^STDSEMVER(s)         — minor component, or "" if invalid
  $$patch^STDSEMVER(s)         — patch component, or "" if invalid
  $$prerelease^STDSEMVER(s)    — prerelease tail, or "" if absent
  $$build^STDSEMVER(s)         — build tail, or "" if absent
  $$compare^STDSEMVER(a,b)     — -1/0/1 per SemVer §11 precedence
  $$matches^STDSEMVER(v,range) — 1 iff v satisfies range

Grammar (SemVer 2.0.0 §2):
  <major>.<minor>.<patch>('-'<prerelease>)?('+'<build>)?
  each numeric part: non-negative integer, no leading zeros (except 0)
  <prerelease>: dot-separated IDs; each is [0-9A-Za-z-]+; numeric IDs
    have no leading zeros; alphanumeric IDs may include hyphens.
  <build>: dot-separated IDs; same charset; numeric leading zeros OK.

Range syntax (npm subset):
  exact:       "1.2.3"
  comparator:  ">"|"<"|">="|"<="|"=" prefixing a SemVer
  caret:       "^1.2.3"  ≡  ">=1.2.3 <2.0.0"
  tilde:       "~1.2.3"  ≡  ">=1.2.3 <1.3.0"
  AND:         space-separated comparators must all hold

valid.synopsis: Return 1 iff s is a valid SemVer 2.0.0 string; else 0.

returns.doc: 1 iff well-formed per SemVer 2.0.0; 0 otherwise

valid.description:
Empty input is invalid. Leading 'v' is NOT accepted (callers
must strip it). A '+' or '-' delimiter present but followed by
an empty tail is invalid.

params.name: v
params.doc: by-ref local; killed then populated as v(1..5)

returns.doc: 1 on success; 0 (and v left empty) on invalid input

parse.description: Numeric parts are stored as integers (no leading zeros surface in v).

major.synopsis: Return the major component; "" if s is invalid.

returns.doc: major component; "" if s is invalid

minor.synopsis: Return the minor component; "" if s is invalid.

returns.doc: minor component; "" if s is invalid

patch.synopsis: Return the patch component; "" if s is invalid.

returns.doc: patch component; "" if s is invalid

prerelease.synopsis: Return the prerelease tail (no leading '-'); "" if absent or invalid.

returns.doc: prerelease tail (no leading '-'); "" if absent or invalid

build.synopsis: Return the build tail (no leading '+'); "" if absent or invalid.

returns.doc: build tail (no leading '+'); "" if absent or invalid
-1 if a<b, 0 if a=b, 1 if a>b; "" if either operand is invalid

compare.description:
Build metadata is ignored per SemVer §10. Prerelease precedence
follows §11.4 (release > prerelease; numeric < alphanumeric).

matches.synopsis: Return 1 iff v satisfies the range expression.

params.name: range
params.doc: range expression (exact, comparator, caret, tilde, AND)

returns.doc: 1 iff v satisfies range; 0 otherwise

matches.description:
range may be a bare SemVer (exact match), a single comparator
('>'/'<'/'>='/'<='/'='-prefixed), a caret/tilde expansion, or
a space-separated AND of comparators.

STDSNAP.synopsis: m-stdlib — Snapshot testing: serialize an M tree, diff against a baseline.
STDSNAP.description:
Public extrinsics:
  $$serialize^STDSNAP(.data)               — canonical text dump of data tree
  save^STDSNAP(path, .data)                — write serialize(data) to path
  $$matches^STDSNAP(path, .data)           — 1 iff serialize(data) equals file content
  asserts^STDSNAP(.pass, .fail, path, .data, desc) — STDASSERT-style integration

Workflow:
  First run: caller calls save() to capture the baseline snapshot.
  Subsequent runs: caller calls matches() (or asserts()) to compare
  the current data against the saved baseline. Mismatches surface as
  STDASSERT failures with a one-line summary.

Canonical format (line-per-leaf):
  (subscripts)=value
where subscripts is a comma-separated M-syntax list (numeric
subscripts unquoted; string subscripts double-quoted with embedded
doubles per M convention) and value is the leaf node's M-quoted
scalar. Numeric values emit unquoted; everything else is wrapped in
"..." with internal " doubled.

Lines are emitted in $ORDER walk order — natural M-collation, which
gives numeric sort for numeric subscripts and string sort for the
rest. The walk is deterministic; two calls on the same tree produce
byte-identical output.

Implementation: $QUERY-driven recursive descent. The `data` formal
binds to the caller's array via pass-by-reference; $QUERY(data) walks
descendants via M's natural collation order.

serialize.synopsis: Walk data tree; return the canonical line-per-leaf dump.
serialize.description:
An empty tree (no defined subscripts) returns "".
Trailing LF is *not* emitted — keeps round-trip clean against
writeFile/readFile, which add and strip a trailing LF.
Example: write $$serialize^STDSNAP(.cfg)

save.synopsis: Write serialize(data) to path. Overwrites if exists.
save.description:
Sets $ECODE=,U-STDFS-OPEN-FAIL, on write failure (propagated
from STDFS).
Example: do save^STDSNAP("snapshots/cfg.snap",.cfg)

matches.synopsis: Return 1 iff serialize(data) equals the file's content.
matches.description:
A missing file returns 0 (no match — first run typically calls
save() to seed the snapshot before relying on matches).
Example: write $$matches^STDSNAP("snapshots/cfg.snap",.cfg)

asserts.signature: do asserts^STDSNAP(p, f, path, data, desc)
asserts.description:
On match: increments p and emits PASS via STDASSERT.
On mismatch: increments f and emits FAIL with a brief diagnostic
noting the snapshot path (full diff is the file vs current text;
caller can shell out to `diff` for the byte-level inspection).
Example: do asserts^STDSNAP(.pass,.fail,"cfg.snap",.cfg,"config matches baseline")
Update mode: when ^STDLIB($job,"stdsnap","update")=1, asserts()
writes the current snapshot to `path` (overwriting any existing
file) and records PASS instead of comparing. Used by m-cli's
`m test --update-snapshots` to regenerate baselines after an
intentional change in test output. Update mode never fails —
a write error still fires the underlying STDFS error trap.

qval.synopsis: M-quote a scalar value: numeric → raw; everything else → "..." with " doubled.

isNumeric.synopsis: True iff v is a non-empty canonical numeric M literal.
isNumeric.description:
Internal — uses M's own canonical-form rule: +v rendered as a
string equals v iff v is already in canonical form.

dq.synopsis: Double every " in s — M string-literal escape.
dq.description: Internal — same routine as STDCSV's dq but inlined for self-containment.

STDSTR.synopsis: m-stdlib — String helpers (pad / trim / split / replaceAll / case / repeat).
STDSTR.description:
Public extrinsics:
  $$pad^STDSTR(s,n,c?)         — alias for padLeft (numeric formatting default)
  $$padLeft^STDSTR(s,n,c?)     — left-pad s to width n with c (default " ")
  $$padRight^STDSTR(s,n,c?)    — right-pad s to width n with c (default " ")
  $$trim^STDSTR(s)             — strip leading and trailing whitespace
  $$trimLeft^STDSTR(s)         — strip leading whitespace only
  $$trimRight^STDSTR(s)        — strip trailing whitespace only
  $$replaceAll^STDSTR(s,find,repl) — replace every non-overlapping occurrence
  $$split^STDSTR(s,sep,.out)   — split on sep; populate out(1..N); return N
  $$startsWith^STDSTR(s,prefix) — predicate: does s begin with prefix?
  $$endsWith^STDSTR(s,suffix)  — predicate: does s end with suffix?
  $$toLowerASCII^STDSTR(s)     — A-Z → a-z (ASCII only; preserves non-alpha)
  $$toUpperASCII^STDSTR(s)     — a-z → A-Z (ASCII only; preserves non-alpha)
  $$repeat^STDSTR(s,n)         — concatenate s with itself n times

Whitespace for trim/trimLeft/trimRight is the four ASCII characters
space ($C(32)), tab ($C(9)), LF ($C(10)), CR ($C(13)). Unicode
whitespace classes (NBSP, ideographic space, etc.) are deliberately
not stripped — keeps trim() byte-faithful and idempotent under any
$ZCHSET mode.

Pure-M throughout: $translate, $piece, $find, $extract, $length —
no STDREGEX dep, no $Z* extensions. Runs unchanged on YDB and IRIS.

pad.synopsis: Alias for padLeft — common numeric-formatting shorthand.

params.name: c
params.doc: fill character (default " "; first char only used)

returns.doc: s left-padded with c to width n

padLeft.synopsis: Left-pad s to width n with c (default " "). Returns s unchanged

params.name: c
params.doc: fill character (default " "; first char only used)

returns.doc: s left-padded; s unchanged if $LENGTH(s) >= n

padLeft.description:
if $LENGTH(s) ≥ n. c may be multi-char; pad is built by char-wise
replication of $EXTRACT(c,1).

padRight.synopsis: Right-pad s to width n with c (default " "). Returns s unchanged

params.name: c
params.doc: fill character (default " "; first char only used)

returns.doc: s right-padded; s unchanged if $LENGTH(s) >= n

trim.synopsis: Strip leading and trailing whitespace (space / tab / LF / CR).

returns.doc: s with outer whitespace stripped

trim.description: Internal whitespace is preserved verbatim. Empty input returns "".

returns.doc: s with leading whitespace stripped
s with trailing whitespace stripped
s with every non-overlapping match replaced

replaceAll.description:
An empty `find` returns s unchanged (no infinite loop). Replacement
is non-recursive — the new bytes inserted by `repl` are not rescanned.
Implementation: $piece-based join — split s on find, rejoin with repl.

split.synopsis: Split s on sep; populate out(1..N); return N.

params.name: out
params.doc: by-ref local; killed then populated as out(1..N)

returns.doc: number of pieces (0 if s="" or sep="")

split.description: Trailing separator yields a trailing empty element; "a,b," → 3 pieces.

startsWith.synopsis: Return 1 iff s begins with prefix; else 0. Empty prefix → 1.

returns.doc: 1 iff s begins with prefix; empty prefix returns 1

endsWith.synopsis: Return 1 iff s ends with suffix; else 0. Empty suffix → 1.

returns.doc: 1 iff s ends with suffix; empty suffix returns 1

toLowerASCII.synopsis: A-Z → a-z; preserves all other characters.

returns.doc: s with A-Z mapped to a-z (other chars unchanged)

toLowerASCII.description:
Operates byte-wise — no locale awareness, no Unicode handling.
For full Unicode case folding wait on a future STDUNICODE.

toUpperASCII.synopsis: a-z → A-Z; preserves all other characters.

returns.doc: s with a-z mapped to A-Z (other chars unchanged)

toUpperASCII.description: Operates byte-wise — no locale awareness, no Unicode handling.

repeat.synopsis: Concatenate s with itself n times. Returns "" for n ≤ 0 or s="".

returns.doc: s concatenated with itself n times

STDTOML.synopsis: m-stdlib — TOML 1.0 parser (deliberately narrow v1 subset).
STDTOML.description:
Public extrinsics:
  $$parse^STDTOML(text,.root) — parse TOML doc into root tree; return 1/0
  $$valid^STDTOML(text)       — predicate: 1 iff parse succeeds
  $$get^STDTOML(.root,key)    — value lookup; "section.key" addresses tables
  $$type^STDTOML(.root,key)   — "string" / "integer" / "float" / "bool" / ""

Tree representation:
  root("v",path)              — value (the M scalar after coercion)
  root("t",path)              — type tag ("string"/"integer"/"float"/"bool")
  path is the dotted address: "k" for top-level, "section.k" for sectioned.

Grammar (TOML 1.0 subset shipped in v1):
  <doc>        ::= (<line> NL)*
  <line>       ::= <blank> | <comment> | <table> | <pair>
  <comment>    ::= "#" .* (whole-line or trailing — stripped before pair parse)
  <table>      ::= "[" <bare-key> "]"
  <pair>       ::= <bare-key> "=" <value>
  <bare-key>   ::= [A-Za-z0-9_-]+
  <value>      ::= <basic-string> | <integer> | <float> | <bool>
  <basic-string> ::= '"' (chars with \n \t \r \" \\ escapes) '"'
  <integer>    ::= "-"? [0-9]+
  <float>      ::= "-"? [0-9]+ "." [0-9]+
  <bool>       ::= "true" | "false"

Out of scope (queued for v0.x.y under T18):
  - arrays, inline tables, dotted keys, [[array-of-tables]]
  - literal strings ('...'), multi-line strings ("""..." or '''...''')
  - integer literals with underscores, hex/oct/bin int prefixes
  - special floats (inf, -inf, nan)
  - exponent notation in floats (1.5e3)
  - datetime values (TOML offset / local datetime / local date / local time)

parse.synopsis: Parse TOML text into root tree; return 1 on success, 0 on parse error.

params.name: text
params.doc: TOML 1.0 document (subset documented in routine header)

returns.doc: 1 on success; 0 on parse error

parse.description:
On failure, root is left in whatever partial state the parse
achieved (caller may want to KILL it).

valid.synopsis: Return 1 iff text parses as valid TOML; else 0.

returns.doc: 1 iff text parses; 0 otherwise

valid.description: Same as parse() but discards the resulting tree.

get.synopsis: Return the value at key (dotted path); "" if absent.

params.name: key
params.doc: dotted path: "k" for top-level; "section.k" for sectioned

returns.doc: the scalar value; "" if key is absent

type.synopsis: Return the type tag at key, or "" if absent.

returns.doc: one of "string", "integer", "float", "bool"; "" if absent

STDURL.synopsis: m-stdlib — RFC 3986 URI parser, builder, encoder, resolver.
STDURL.description:
Seven public extrinsics:
  $$parse^STDURL(url,.parts)     — split URL into components; → 1/0
  $$build^STDURL(.parts)         — assemble URL from components
  $$encode^STDURL(s,safe)        — percent-encode (RFC 3986 §2.1)
  $$decode^STDURL(s)             — percent-decode (lenient — leaves
                                   malformed % as literal text)
  $$valid^STDURL(url)            — RFC 3986 well-formedness predicate
  $$normalize^STDURL(url)        — case + percent + dot-segment normalization
  $$resolve^STDURL(base,ref)     — resolve a relative reference (§5.3)

The .parts array is a flat namespace with these keys:
  parts("scheme")     parts("userinfo")   parts("host")
  parts("port")       parts("path")       parts("query")
  parts("fragment")
parse() initialises every key (empty when absent) so callers can
index without $get.

Strict-mode resolve: a same-scheme reference like "http:g" against
base "http://a/b/c/d;p?q" resolves to "http:g" (RFC 3986 §5.3
strict; non-strict back-compat is not implemented).

valid() is the strict gate. decode() is intentionally lenient
(matches Python urllib.parse.unquote) — bad %-sequences pass through
as literal text. Use valid() first if strict input is required.

Input is treated as a string of bytes (one M character per byte —
values 0..255 via $ASCII / $CHAR). RFC 3986 §2.5 specifies UTF-8
for non-ASCII; producers/consumers should percent-encode UTF-8
bytes before passing to encode().

parse.description:
The caller's parts array is killed before population so stale
subscripts do not leak across calls. All seven keys are
written; absent components are set to "". Empty input is
valid (a degenerate same-document reference) and yields all-
empty parts.
Example: do parse^STDURL("https://example.com/foo?x=1",.p)

build.synopsis: Reassemble parts into a URL string.
build.description:
Emits authority ("//user@host:port") iff any of userinfo, host,
or port is non-empty. Empty components are omitted. The result
round-trips parse() for canonical inputs.
Example: set p("scheme")="https",p("host")="x.com" w $$build^STDURL(.p)

encode.synopsis: Percent-encode s. Unreserved chars + chars in safe pass through.
encode.description:
Unreserved set is ALPHA / DIGIT / "-" / "." / "_" / "~"
(RFC 3986 §2.3). Pass safe="/" for path components, safe="="
for query keys, etc. Space becomes %20 (per RFC; not "+", which
is the application/x-www-form-urlencoded convention).
Example: write $$encode^STDURL("hello world","")  ; "hello%20world"

decode.synopsis: Percent-decode all valid %HH; leave malformed % as literal.
decode.description:
Lenient — "%2", "%2G", and bare "%" pass through unchanged.
Use valid() first if strict input is required. "+" is treated
as a literal "+" (form-encoding semantics belong to the caller).
Example: write $$decode^STDURL("hello%20world")  ; "hello world"

valid.synopsis: True iff url is a well-formed RFC 3986 URI (or relative reference).
valid.description:
Empty string is valid (a degenerate same-document reference).
Rejects raw spaces, control characters, and malformed %HH.
If a colon appears before any '/?#', the prefix is checked as
a scheme (ALPHA *(ALPHA/DIGIT/+/-/.)).
Example: write $$valid^STDURL("/foo")  ; 1

normalize.description:
Lowercases the scheme (§6.2.2.1), lowercases the host
(§3.2.2), uppercases hex digits in %HH, percent-decodes
unreserved characters (§6.2.2.2), and removes . and ..
dot-segments from the path (§6.2.2.3 / §5.2.4).
Example: write $$normalize^STDURL("HTTPS://EX.COM/a/./b")
; "https://ex.com/a/b"

resolve.synopsis: Resolve ref against base per RFC 3986 §5.3 (strict mode).
resolve.description:
Returns the absolute URI string. Strict mode means a reference
that begins with the same scheme as base is still treated as
scheme-bearing (e.g. "http:g" against "http://a/b/c/" → "http:g").
Example: write $$resolve^STDURL("http://a/b/c/d","../g")
; "http://a/b/g"

parseAuth.synopsis: Split authority into userinfo / host / port.

tryScheme.synopsis: → 1 iff s starts with a valid scheme; sets scheme.
tryScheme.description:
Internal — RFC 3986 §3.1 scheme = ALPHA *(ALPHA/DIGIT/"+"/"-"/".").
A '/' before the first ':' disqualifies (no scheme).

findPathStart.synopsis: → 1-based index of first '/' in s, or length(s)+1 if none.
findPathStart.description: Internal — used by parse() to bound the authority.

hasAuth.synopsis: → 1 if any of userinfo / host / port is non-empty.
hasAuth.description:
Internal — pragmatic stand-in for "authority defined" (RFC 3986
distinguishes empty authority from absent; we conflate them).

mergePath.description:
Internal — when base has authority and base path is empty,
returns "/" + refPath; otherwise replaces the last segment of
basePath (everything after the last '/') with refPath.

removeDots.description:
Internal — collapses ".", "..", and any "/." or "/.." segments
against the accumulated output. Required by both normalize()
and resolve() to canonicalise hierarchical paths.

normPct.synopsis: Decode %HH for unreserved chars; uppercase hex of others.
normPct.description: Internal — used by normalize() per RFC 3986 §6.2.2.

pct.synopsis: → "%HH" for byte value b (0..255). Hex is uppercase per §6.2.2.1.
pct.description: Internal — render one byte as a percent-triplet.

hex2dec.description: Internal — two-digit hex pair to integer 0..255.

isHex.synopsis: → 1 if s is non-empty and all hex digits (any case).
isHex.description: Internal — single $translate validates the whole string.

isAlpha.synopsis: → 1 if c is one ASCII letter.

isUriChar.synopsis: → 1 if c is an unreserved or reserved URI character.

lower.description: Internal — used by normalize() for scheme and host.

upper.description: Internal — used by normPct() for percent-encoded hex digits.

STDUUID.description:
Five public extrinsics:
  $$v4^STDUUID()         — random UUID v4 ("xxxxxxxx-xxxx-4xxx-yxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx")
  $$v7^STDUUID()         — time-ordered UUID v7
  $$valid^STDUUID(u)     — 1 if u is canonical 36-char hex form
  $$version^STDUUID(u)   — integer 1..15 from the version nibble, "" if invalid
  $$variant^STDUUID(u)   — "ncs" | "rfc4122" | "microsoft" | "future" | ""

v4 randomness is from $RANDOM (Mersenne Twister) — NOT cryptographically
strong. Adequate for distributed primary keys; do not use for tokens.
v7 timestamp is ms since 1970-01-01 UTC (48 bits): high 48 bits encode
the timestamp so byte-wise sort = generation order.

All output is lowercase hex per RFC 9562 §4 recommendation.

v4.synopsis: Return a new RFC-4122 v4 UUID.

returns.doc: canonical 36-char hex UUID v4 (lowercase, hyphenated)

v4.description:
122 bits of randomness; version nibble='4'; variant nibble in 8/9/a/b.
Randomness is from $RANDOM (Mersenne Twister) — adequate for distributed
primary keys; not cryptographically strong (do not use for tokens).

v7.synopsis: Return a new RFC-9562 v7 UUID (time-ordered).

returns.doc: canonical 36-char hex UUID v7; byte-wise sort = generation order

examples: set id=$$v7^STDUUID()  ; sorts in generation order
v7.description:
First 48 bits = ms-since-Unix-epoch (sortable). 12-bit rand_a,
variant nibble (8/9/a/b), 12-bit rand_b, 50-bit rand_c.

valid.synopsis: Return 1 iff u is a canonical 36-char hex UUID; else 0.

returns.doc: 1 iff canonical 36-char hex; 0 otherwise

valid.description:
Accepts both lowercase and uppercase hex. Hyphens must sit
at exactly positions 9, 14, 19, 24.

version.synopsis: Return integer version (1..15) from position 15, or "" if invalid.

returns.doc: 1..15 from the version nibble; "" if `u` is not valid

version.description: For a v4 UUID this returns 4; for v7, 7. Empty string for malformed.

variant.synopsis: Classify UUID variant from the high bits of position 20.

returns.doc: one of "ncs", "rfc4122", "microsoft", "future"; "" if `u` invalid

variant.description:
"ncs" (high bit 0), "rfc4122" (high 10), "microsoft" (high 110),
"future" (high 111). Empty string for malformed input.

STDXFRM.synopsis: m-stdlib — Higher-order array transforms (map / filter / reduce via @-indirection lambdas).
STDXFRM.description:
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-036
M-MOD-036 flags M's `@` indirection on a "tainted" local — but
XECUTE-via-@ of a caller-supplied expression IS the contract of
this module: the lambda is the abstraction. Same shape as STDMOCK's
`do @resolved@(.args)` and STDFIX's `xecute @cleanupCmd`.

Public entry points:
  do map^STDXFRM(.in, expr, .out)      — out(k) := <expr> for each k
  do filter^STDXFRM(.in, expr, .out)   — copy in(k)→out(k) iff <expr>
  $$reduce^STDXFRM(.in, expr, init)    — fold left; <expr> is new acc

The lambda string `expr` is evaluated via `@expr` in this module's
stack frame, so it sees these locals:
  value  — the current element's value (in(k))
  key    — the current subscript (k)
  acc    — (reduce only) the accumulator carried forward

Walk discipline:
  - $ORDER-walk at depth 1 only (the canonical "1-D vector" shape).
  - Subscript shape doesn't matter — int, string, sparse all work.
  - For map/filter, `out` is killed before the walk so stale
    leftovers from a prior call do not leak through.
  - For reduce, the empty-input case returns `init` unchanged.

Error semantics: if `expr` raises (compile error, $ECODE set,
division by zero, etc.) the error propagates to the caller's
$ETRAP unmodified. STDXFRM does not catch.

Pure-M throughout — no $Z* extensions. Runs unchanged on YDB and
IRIS. The @-indirection idiom is ANSI standard since the M[UMPS]
X11.1 standard.

map.description:
Example:
new a,out  set a(1)=1,a(2)=2,a(3)=3
do map^STDXFRM(.a, "value*2", .out)
; out(1)=2, out(2)=4, out(3)=6
Lambda locals visible to expr: `value` (in(k)) and `key` (k).

filter.description:
Example:
new a,out  set a(1)=5,a(2)=15,a(3)=8,a(4)=20
do filter^STDXFRM(.a, "value>10", .out)
; out(2)=15, out(4)=20 (others dropped)
Subscripts are preserved. Values are copied verbatim — the
predicate result is discarded after the keep/drop decision.

reduce.synopsis: Fold left: walk in, evaluate expr with `acc`+`value`+`key`.
reduce.description:
Returns the final accumulator. Empty `in` returns `init` unchanged.
Example:
new a  set a(1)=1,a(2)=2,a(3)=3,a(4)=4
write $$reduce^STDXFRM(.a, "acc+value", 0)  ; 10
write $$reduce^STDXFRM(.a, "acc*value", 1)  ; 24

STDXML.synopsis: m-stdlib — XML parser (well-formed XML 1.0 subset, in-progress).
STDXML.description:
m-lint: disable-file=M-MOD-036
M-MOD-036 flags M's `@` indirection on a "tainted" local. The
XPath helpers (T27) build subscript references from path strings
that are 100% generated from internal `for i=1:1:childCount` loops
— no user input flows into the indirection target. The pattern is
documented in `docs/modules/stdxml.md` "T27 path-walk indirection".

Public extrinsics (v0):
  $$parse^STDXML(text,.root)              — parse text into root tree; 1/0
  $$valid^STDXML(text)                    — predicate
  $$rootName^STDXML(.node)                — element tag name
  $$attr^STDXML(.node, name)              — attribute value or ""
  $$text^STDXML(.node)                    — direct text content
  $$childCount^STDXML(.node)              — count of element children
  $$childByName^STDXML(.node, name, .out) — first child with name → .out; 1/0
  $$lastError^STDXML()                    — diagnostic string or ""

Tree shape (caller-owned; pass by reference):
  node("name")           — element tag
  node("attr", attrName) — attribute value (decoded)
  node("text")           — direct text content (decoded; concatenated text nodes)
  node("childCount")     — number of element children
  node("child", n)       — n-th child element (recursive structure)

Child traversal MUST go through `childByName` (or analogous helpers
that internally `merge` the child subtree to a non-subscripted local).
Direct passing of `.node("child", n)` is **invalid YDB syntax** —
pass-by-reference of a subscripted local is disallowed at compile
time (TOOLCHAIN-FINDINGS row 2026-05-06, demoted to docs 2026-05-07).
The merge-then-pass idiom is canonical for any STDXML descent.

Grammar (v0 subset of XML 1.0):
  <document>   ::= <ws>? <element> <ws>?
  <element>    ::= <empty-tag> | <stag> <content> <etag>
  <empty-tag>  ::= "<" <name> <attrs>? <ws>? "/>"
  <stag>       ::= "<" <name> <attrs>? <ws>? ">"
  <etag>       ::= "</" <name> <ws>? ">"
  <attrs>      ::= ( <ws> <attr> )+
  <attr>       ::= <name> <ws>? "=" <ws>? <attval>
  <attval>     ::= '"' <chardata> '"' | "'" <chardata> "'"
  <content>    ::= <chardata>? ( <element> <chardata>? )*
  <name>       ::= [A-Za-z_:] [A-Za-z0-9_:.-]*
  <chardata>   ::= text with the 5 standard entities decoded

T26 — DTDs / DOCTYPE / internal subset / custom entities (closed
2026-05-08): `<!DOCTYPE root [...]>` is consumed at the prolog;
`<!ENTITY name "value">` declarations populate ctx("entity",name)
and expand in subsequent text + attribute content. `<!ELEMENT>` /
`<!ATTLIST>` / `<!NOTATION>` decls are parsed but not enforced
(skipped through the next `>`). External DTDs (`SYSTEM "url"`,
`PUBLIC "id" "url"`) are accepted in the prolog and silently
ignored — only the internal subset is materialised. Parameter
entities (`%name;`) and external entity references are out of
scope; if a real consumer needs them, lift through a follow-up.

parse.synopsis: Parse text into root tree; return 1/0.
parse.description:
Sets ^STDLIB($job,"stdxml","err") with a diagnostic on failure.
Example: do  set rc=$$parse^STDXML(text,.tree)

valid.synopsis: Return 1 iff text parses as valid XML; else 0.

rootName.synopsis: Return the element tag name; "" if missing.

ns.synopsis: Return the namespace URI for the element; "" if not in any namespace.
ns.description:
T25 — uses xmlns / xmlns:prefix declarations in scope at the
element's position. Inherited from the nearest enclosing
declaration unless shadowed.
Example: write $$ns^STDXML(.tree)  ; "urn:hl7-org:v3"

attrNs.synopsis: Return the namespace URI for an attribute; "" if unprefixed or absent.
attrNs.description:
T25b — per XML Namespaces 1.0 §6.2, the default xmlns does
NOT apply to unprefixed attributes; only attributes with an
explicit prefix carry a namespace URI. The built-in `xml:`
prefix resolves to `http://www.w3.org/XML/1998/namespace`.
Example: write $$attrNs^STDXML(.tree,"xsi:type")

xpath.synopsis: Run an XPath query; populate results(1..N); return N.
xpath.description:
Supports element paths (`a/b/c`), absolute (`/foo`),
descendant axis (`//x`), 1-based position predicates
(`x[1]`), wildcards (`*` and `@*`), and attribute axis
(`@attr`). Attribute matches surface as result entries
with `results(i,"text")` set to the attribute value and
`results(i,"name")` set to the attribute name — so
`xpathText` returns the attribute value transparently.
T27b — predicate expressions also accept comparison
operators (`=`, `!=`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`) and the
functions `position()`, `last()`, `name()`, `text()`,
`count()`, `string-length()`, `normalize-space()`,
`contains()`, `starts-with()`. Examples: `a[@id='2']`,
`*[name()='b']`, `book[count(author)>1]`.
Returns 0 (with results killed) for an unparseable expression.
Example: do  set n=$$xpath^STDXML(.doc,"/r/items/item[2]",.r)

xpathOne.description:
Convenience wrapper over xpath.
Example: do  if $$xpathOne^STDXML(.doc,"/r/title",.t) ...

xpathText.synopsis: Return the direct text of the first match; "" if none.

text.synopsis: Return direct text content; "" if no text.

childCount.synopsis: Return number of element children; 0 if none.

childByName.synopsis: Find first child with `name`; merge into `.out`. 1/0.
childByName.description:
Pass-by-ref of `.out` allows the caller to receive the child
subtree without violating YDB's `.x(SUBS)` syntax limit. The
merge under the hood is what makes recursive descent into a
parsed XML tree work in YDB.
Example: do  if $$childByName^STDXML(.tree,"book",.b) ...

lastError.synopsis: Return the last parse error diagnostic; "" if none / parse succeeded.

initCtx.description: Internal — pos is the 1-based current position.

peek.synopsis: Return the character at the current position; "" at EOF.

peekN.synopsis: Return the next n characters from the current position.
peekN.description: Internal — for matching multi-char tokens like "</".

advance.synopsis: Advance the position by n characters.

parseElement.synopsis: Parse one element. nsIn is the inherited namespace map.
parseElement.description:
Internal — leaves the context positioned after the element.
Threads a per-element namespace map (T25). The parent's `nsIn`
is copied into a local `myNs` before modification, so children
see this element's xmlns declarations but the parent does not.

parseAttrs.description:
Internal — leaves the context at the next non-whitespace
character (typically `>` or `/>`).

parseAttrValue.synopsis: Read characters until the matching quote (no escapes — entities decoded later).

parseContent.description:
Internal — populates node("text") and node("child", n, ...).
Dispatches on `<!--` (comment), `<![CDATA[` (literal text),
`<?` (PI, skip), `</` (end of content), and `<name` (child).
nsIn is the inherited namespace map, threaded through to each
child element via parseElement.

absorbXmlns.synopsis: Pull `xmlns` / `xmlns:prefix` attrs out of node into nsMap.
absorbXmlns.description:
Internal — driven by parseElement. Walks node("attr",...),
collects the namespace declarations into a temporary list,
applies them to nsMap, and kills them from node("attr",...).

parseXPath.synopsis: Parse XPath expression into steps(1..N) with axis/name/pred/attrName.
parseXPath.description:
Internal — supports `name`, `name1/name2`, `/name`, `//name`,
`name[N]`, the wildcard `*` (T27a), and the attribute axis
`@attrName` / `@*` (T27a). An attribute step is terminal —
nothing may follow it. T27b — predicates may be either a
positive integer (legacy `[N]` position filter) or a full
comparison expression like `[@id='2']` / `[name()='foo']` /
`[count(child)>3]`.
Returns 1 on success, 0 on parse failure.

applyStep.signature: do applyStep^STDXML(tree, steps, stepIdx, paths, pathCount, newPaths, newCount)
applyStep.description:
Apply one XPath step to the current candidate set, producing
a new candidate set in newPaths(1..newCount). T27a: when the
step is an attribute axis (`@x`), candidates are sourced per
the axis (child=basePath, descendant=basePath's descendants)
and each candidate's matching attribute(s) become results.
T27b: predKind="expr" routes through applyExprPredicate for
per-candidate expression evaluation; predKind="num" keeps the
legacy O(1) position filter via applyPredicate.

applyPredicate.description:
Internal — uses merge so any subnodes (attribute results)
are preserved through the in-place reduction.

matchName.synopsis: 1 if the node at `path` matches `want` (`*` is wildcard).
matchName.description:
Internal — used by axis=absolute and the wildcard-aware
child / descendant collectors.

nameAtPath.synopsis: Return the name of the node at the given path; "" if none.
nameAtPath.description: Internal — path is comma-separated list of child indices.

buildRef.synopsis: Build an M name reference like `tree("child",1,"child",3,"name")`.
buildRef.description:
Internal — `path` is a comma-separated list of child indices
(or "" for the root). `suffix` is the trailing subscript term
(or "" for none). Produces a single subscript list inside the
outer parens — chaining subscripts is not valid M syntax, so
helpers must concatenate everything within one ref.

collectChildren.signature: do collectChildren^STDXML(tree, basePath, name, newPaths, newCount)
collectChildren.description:
Append paths to immediate children of basePath whose name
matches. `name="*"` is a wildcard (any element).

collectDescendants.signature: do collectDescendants^STDXML(tree, basePath, name, newPaths, newCount)
collectDescendants.description:
Walk all descendants of basePath; append matches by name.
`name="*"` is a wildcard (any element). Descendant-only —
does NOT include the basePath node itself; matches strict
XPath descendant axis semantics.

collectAttribute.signature: do collectAttribute^STDXML(tree, basePath, attrName, newPaths, newCount)
collectAttribute.description:
T27a — attribute axis terminal step. `attrName="*"` matches
every attribute on the element at basePath; otherwise only
the named attribute (if present). Each match writes
newPaths(idx) = basePath plus subnodes "attrValue" / "attrName"
which mergePathToResult lifts into results(idx,"text") /
results(idx,"name").

mergePathToResult.description:
Lift one path entry into results(idx). Attribute matches
surface as scalar-like results: results(idx,"text")=value
and results(idx,"name")=attrName. Element matches merge
the subtree at paths(idx) so callers walk them like any
parsed-tree node.

applyExprPredicate.description:
T27b — for each candidate path, evaluate the predicate
expression and keep the candidate iff the result coerces to
boolean true (XPath 1.0 truthiness: non-empty string,
non-zero number, true bool). The candidate sub-tree
(attrValue / attrName for attribute matches) is preserved
through `merge` so attribute-axis predicates survive.

parsePredExpr.synopsis: Parse a predicate body into an AST.
parsePredExpr.description:
Internal — predStr is the content between `[` and `]`.
AST shape: ast("kind") ∈ {num, str, attr, attrAll, bareName,
wildcard, call, binop}. Numeric/string carry ("val");
attr/bareName/call carry ("name"); call adds ("argCount") and
("arg",i) sub-trees; binop adds ("op"), ("lhs"), ("rhs")
sub-trees. Returns 1 on success, 0 on parse failure.

parseExpr.description:
Internal — single-level comparison only (no chained `<`).
Operators: `=`, `!=`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`.

parsePrimary.description:
Internal — string lit / number / `@name` or `@*` / parenthesised
expression / `*` (wildcard, only meaningful as count() arg) /
bare name (used in count() arg) / function call `name(args)`.

parseStringLit.signature: do parseStringLit^STDXML(s, pos, len, quote, ast, bad)
parseStringLit.description: Internal — `quote` is the matched delimiter (`'` or `"`).

parseNumLit.signature: do parseNumLit^STDXML(s, pos, len, ast, bad)
parseNumLit.description: Internal — integer or decimal (no exponent / sign).

parseAttrRef.signature: do parseAttrRef^STDXML(s, pos, len, ast, bad)

parseParen.signature: do parseParen^STDXML(s, pos, len, ast, bad)
parseParen.description:
Internal — `(` expr `)`. Sub-expr is parsed via parseExpr so
comparison nesting is allowed inside the group.

parseFnOrName.signature: do parseFnOrName^STDXML(s, pos, len, ast, bad)
parseFnOrName.description:
Internal — name optionally followed by `(args)`. Bare name is
an XPath relative name reference (used as a count() arg).

readNameToken.description:
Internal — read a name [A-Za-z_:][A-Za-z0-9_:.-]*. Hyphen is
included so XPath function names like `string-length` parse.

skipExprWs.description: Internal — advance past space / tab / CR / LF.

evalPredExpr.signature: do evalPredExpr^STDXML(tree, ctxPath, posInSet, sizeOfSet, ast, outType, outVal)
evalPredExpr.description:
Internal — evaluate the AST against the candidate at ctxPath.
Sets outType ∈ {"num","str","bool"} and outVal accordingly.

evalCall.signature: do evalCall^STDXML(tree, ctxPath, posInSet, sizeOfSet, ast, outType, outVal)
evalCall.description:
Internal — function-call dispatch. Unknown functions return
bool 0 (truthiness preserved as falsy).

evalCountArg.description:
Internal — count() takes a node-set producer. v0 supports a
single-step relative path: bareName / `*` / `@name` / `@*`.

attrLookupAt.signature: do attrLookupAt^STDXML(tree, path, attrName, exists, val)
attrLookupAt.description: Internal — set exists/val for an attribute lookup at path.

textAtPath.description: Internal — direct text content at path; "" if absent.

compareOp.signature: do compareOp^STDXML(op, lT, lV, rT, rV, outType, outVal)
compareOp.description:
Internal — `=`/`!=` use string equality unless both operands
are numeric; `<`/`>`/`<=`/`>=` always coerce both to number.

normWs.description:
Internal — XPath normalize-space: trim leading/trailing
whitespace and collapse internal runs to a single space.

resolveAttrNs.synopsis: Resolve namespace URIs for any prefixed attrs on node.
resolveAttrNs.description:
T25b — internal. Walks node("attr",...); for each attr name
containing ":", splits into prefix:local, resolves the prefix
against nsMap (with the built-in `xml:` prefix as a fallback),
and stores the resolved URI at node("attrNs", attrName).
Unprefixed attrs get no entry in node("attrNs",...) — per spec,
they have no namespace regardless of any default xmlns.
Returns 0 (and sets err) if any prefix is undeclared.

splitQName.description:
Internal — handles the no-colon case ("foo" → prefix="" / local="foo").
A trailing colon ("x:") is malformed but treated leniently —
prefix="x", local="" — caller will likely reject downstream.

parseName.synopsis: Read an XML name [A-Za-z_:] [A-Za-z0-9_:.-]*; return "" on failure.

skipDocLevel.synopsis: Skip whitespace, comments, PIs, and DOCTYPE at the document level.
skipDocLevel.description:
Internal — used before and after the root element. Returns 0
only if a comment / PI / DOCTYPE is malformed (unclosed);
whitespace and a missing item are normal. DOCTYPE handling
populates ctx("entity",name) for any internal `<!ENTITY>`
declarations, which `decodeEntities` then expands.

parseDoctype.description:
Internal — T26. External SYSTEM / PUBLIC identifiers are
tolerated but ignored; only the internal subset (between
`[` and `]`) is parsed for `<!ENTITY>` decls.

parseDoctypeSubset.synopsis: Inside `[...]` of DOCTYPE — consume markup decls until `]`.
parseDoctypeSubset.description:
Internal — T26. Recognised forms: `<!ENTITY>` (kept),
`<!ELEMENT>` / `<!ATTLIST>` / `<!NOTATION>` (skipped),
`<!--…-->` (skipped), `<?…?>` (skipped). Whitespace
between decls is consumed. Parameter entity references
(`%name;`) are not supported.

parseEntityDecl.synopsis: Consume `<!ENTITY name "value">` and record in ctx("entity",name).
parseEntityDecl.description:
Internal — T26. Single- or double-quoted values; entity
refs inside the value are NOT recursively expanded in v1
(consumer-driven simplification — modern XML rarely nests
custom-entity definitions).

skipMarkupDecl.description:
Internal — T26. v1 does not enforce content models; the
declaration is consumed verbatim. Inner SYSTEM / PUBLIC
literals are tolerated.

skipComment.description:
Internal — XML 1.0 §2.5. Comments may not contain `--` per the
spec, but v0 doesn't enforce that (just searches for `-->`).

skipPI.description:
Internal — XML 1.0 §2.6. Also handles the `<?xml ... ?>`
declaration in the same path (not specially distinguished in v0).

parseCdata.synopsis: Consume `<![CDATA[ ... ]]>`. Append literal content to text.

decodeEntities.synopsis: Decode the 5 standard entities + numeric refs + custom entities in s.
decodeEntities.description:
Internal — & < > " ' → & < > " '.
T24: also &#NNN; (decimal) and &#xHH; (hex), UTF-8-encoded.
T26: any name found in ctx("entity",name) (populated by
parseEntityDecl from the DOCTYPE internal subset) expands
to its declared value. Unknown / undeclared entity refs
pass through verbatim — same lenient policy as v0.

decodeNumericRef.synopsis: Parse `#NNN` (decimal) or `#xHH` (hex). Return code point or -1.
decodeNumericRef.description:
Internal — driven by decodeEntities. `name` excludes the
leading `&` and trailing `;`.

hexDigit.synopsis: Return numeric value of a hex digit; -1 if invalid.

encodeUtf8.synopsis: Encode a code point as a 1-4-byte UTF-8 string.
